name: Albania
overview: Albania is a small Balkan nation on the Adriatic and Ionian coasts of southeastern Europe, bordered by Montenegro, Kosovo, North Macedonia, and Greece. Once one of the most isolated countries in the world under communist rule until 1991, it has since opened significantly to the wider world. The country is known for its ancient Illyrian heritage, its distinctive double-headed eagle national symbol, and its well-preserved Ottoman-era cities such as UNESCO-listed Gjirokastër.
subTitle: 12 Counties

history.label: Current flag
history.note: After communism fell, the red star was removed on 7 April 1992, leaving the plain black double-headed eagle on red. The eagle derives from the 15th-century seal of national hero Skanderbeg.

history.label: Communist (red star)
history.note: Enver Hoxha's People's (later Socialist) Republic topped the eagle with a gold-edged red star, which flew through nearly five decades of hardline Stalinist isolation.

history.label: German occupation
history.note: After Italy capitulated in 1943, Nazi Germany occupied Albania and installed a puppet regime that flew the plain black eagle on red — stripped of the fascist fasces — while presenting itself as "independent." Communist-led partisans controlled much of the country and drove the Germans out by late 1944.

history.label: Italian protectorate
history.note: Under Italian occupation the eagle was flanked by two fascist fasces, replacing the Zog crown and tying Albania's symbol to Mussolini's regime.

history.label: Kingdom of Zog
history.note: When Ahmet Zogu proclaimed himself King Zog I, a royal crown was placed above the double-headed eagle and the monarchy styled itself the Kingdom of Albania.

history.label: Albanian Republic
history.note: Between the principality and the monarchy, Ahmet Zogu first ruled as president of a short-lived Albanian Republic, whose flag placed the black double-headed eagle on red beneath a helmet — the helmet of Skanderbeg, the 15th-century national hero. Zogu dissolved the republic in 1928 to crown himself king.

history.label: Principality of Albania
history.note: The short-lived principality under Prince Wilhelm of Wied flew the black double-headed eagle on red, marking Albania's first modern statehood.

predecessors.name: Ottoman Empire
predecessors.note: The red field with white crescent and star was standardized in 1844; older Ottoman banners varied widely and often carried multiple crescents.

predecessors.name: Principality of Albania (1914)
predecessors.note: The brief principality under Prince Wilhelm of Wied: a deep-red field with a large black double-headed eagle, established just after Albanian independence.

predecessors.name: Republic of Central Albania
predecessors.note: Warlord Essad Pasha Toptani's short-lived statelet around Durrës during Albania's chaotic birth, flying a red flag with a white star.

name: Andorra
overview: Andorra is a tiny microstate nestled in the Pyrenean mountains between France and Spain, one of Europe's smallest countries. It has been jointly governed as a co-principality by the French head of state and the Bishop of Urgell since 1278, an arrangement unique in the modern world. Despite its small size, Andorra is a popular destination known for duty-free shopping, skiing, and spectacular mountain scenery.
subTitle: 7 Parishes

history.label: Tricolor with arms
history.note: The vertical blue-yellow-red tricolor was introduced in 1866, its design popularly attributed to Napoleon III, one of Andorra's two co-princes. It was officially codified in 1971 and standardized in 1993.

history.label: Red-yellow bicolour
history.note: Before the tricolor, Andorra used a plain two-band flag of red and yellow — the colors of the neighboring county of Foix and the Crown of Aragon — with no coat of arms.

name: Austria
overview: Austria is a landlocked country in Central Europe known for its alpine scenery, imperial architecture, and extraordinary musical heritage. For centuries it was the centre of the Habsburg Empire, one of Europe's most powerful dynasties, which shaped the culture of the entire continent. Vienna, the capital, was home to Mozart, Beethoven, Schubert, Brahms, and Mahler, and remains one of the world's preeminent cultural cities.
subTitle: 9 States

history.label: Austria (Second Republic)
history.note: With independence restored in 1945, Austria reinstated the red-white-red triband. Legend ties it to a medieval Babenberg duke whose white tunic was left red but for the band under his belt — one of the oldest flag designs still in use.

history.label: Anschluss (annexed by Nazi Germany)
history.note: Following the Anschluss of March 1938, Austria ceased to exist as an independent state and the Nazi German flag flew over its territory until liberation in 1945.

history.label: Federal State (Ständestaat)
history.note: The authoritarian Austrofascist Federal State kept the red-white-red bands but added a state emblem with a double-headed eagle and the crutch-cross. It ended with the Nazi annexation.

history.label: First Austrian Republic
history.note: After the empire collapsed, the new republic adopted the red-white-red triband that is essentially the modern Austrian flag.

history.label: Austria-Hungary
history.note: The Dual Monarchy used a combined civil ensign joining the Austrian red-white-red with the Hungarian colors, while the black-gold Habsburg flag remained in dynastic use until 1918.

predecessors.name: Duchy of Styria
predecessors.note: The white-over-green Landesfarben of the Habsburg duchy; its arms bear a white fire-breathing panther. Styria is now split between Austria and Slovenia.

predecessors.name: Duchy of Carinthia
predecessors.note: The yellow-red-white crown-land flag of one of the oldest duchies of the Holy Roman Empire, whose arms show three black lions on gold.

name: Belarus
overview: Belarus is a landlocked country in Eastern Europe bordered by Russia, Ukraine, Poland, Lithuania, and Latvia, maintaining close political and economic ties with Russia. Minsk, the capital, was almost entirely destroyed during World War II and was meticulously rebuilt in an imposing Soviet architectural style with wide boulevards and grand public squares. The country has a rich tradition of folk art, weaving, and woodcraft.
subTitle: 7 Oblasts

history.label: Flag of Belarus
history.note: A 2012 standardization made the hoist ornament red-on-white. The red-green bicolour is one of the few national flags that still keeps a Soviet-derived design rather than reverting after independence.

history.label: Red-green flag (1995)
history.note: Introduced after a 1995 referendum, this revived the Soviet-era look without the communist symbols. The hoist ornament was originally rendered white-on-red.

history.label: White-red-white flag
history.note: First used by the 1918 Belarusian People's Republic and readopted at independence in 1991. Dropped after the 1995 referendum, it is now the symbol of the Belarusian democratic opposition.

history.label: Byelorussian SSR
history.note: A red field with a green bottom stripe and a red-and-white ornamental pattern at the hoist. Adopted in 1951, it directly inspired the flag Belarus flies today.

predecessors.name: Russian Empire
predecessors.note: The black-yellow-white 'imperial colors' were drawn from the state coat of arms by Alexander II, before Nicholas II demoted them for the white-blue-red tricolor.

predecessors.name: Soviet Union
predecessors.note: The gold hammer and sickle beneath a red star stood for the worker-peasant alliance. The exact shade and proportions were fixed by Soviet law in 1955.

predecessors.name: Byelorussian SSR
predecessors.note: Red over a green band with a white folk-ornament stripe at the hoist; modern Belarus readopted a near-identical design in 1995, dropping only the hammer and sickle.

predecessors.name: Belarusian People's Republic
predecessors.note: The white-red-white flag of the short-lived republic declared during WWI; suppressed by the Bolsheviks, it returned as independent Belarus's flag from 1991 to 1995.

name: Belgium
overview: Belgium is a small but densely populated country in Western Europe that serves as the de facto capital of the European Union, hosting the EU's major institutions in Brussels. The country is divided into three linguistic communities — Dutch-speaking Flanders, French-speaking Wallonia, and a small German-speaking area — giving it a complex federal structure. Belgium is celebrated globally for its chocolate, beer, waffles, and its rich tradition of comic art.
subTitle: 11 Provinces & Regions

history.label: Kingdom of Belgium
history.note: Belgium's vertical black-yellow-red tricolor was adopted at independence in 1831. The colours derive from the ducal arms of Brabant — a golden lion on black — combined with red from the arms of Hainaut. It's unusually tall and narrow compared to most flags (13:15 ratio), and the black stripe is on the hoist side — the only national flag with black in that position.

history.label: Under the Kingdom of the Netherlands
history.note: After Napoleon's defeat, the Congress of Vienna merged the Austrian Netherlands (roughly modern Belgium) and the Dutch Republic into the United Kingdom of the Netherlands under King William I. The Dutch orange-white-blue flag (displayed as red-white-blue) flew over Belgian territory. The forced union proved deeply unpopular — language, religion, and economic policies all divided north from south, leading to the Belgian Revolution of 1830.

history.label: Brabantine Revolution
history.note: The 1789 Brabantine Revolution against Austrian rule briefly created the United Belgian States. The revolutionary flag took the black, yellow, and red from the coat of arms of the Duchy of Brabant and arranged them horizontally (black on top, as in the Brabant arms). After Belgium's eventual independence in 1830, the revolutionary tradition was honoured but the stripes were turned vertical — creating today's distinctive flag.

predecessors.name: Neutral Moresnet
predecessors.note: A neutral zinc-mining territory jointly administered by Prussia and the Netherlands (later Belgium) after the Congress of Vienna. Its flag is a black-white-blue tricolor.

predecessors.name: United Belgian States
predecessors.note: A short-lived confederation born of the Brabant Revolution against Austrian rule; its black-yellow-red colors were the direct forerunner of the modern Belgian flag.

name: Bosnia and Herzegovina
overview: Bosnia and Herzegovina is a country in the western Balkans with a complex administrative structure established by the 1995 Dayton Agreement following the Bosnian War. The country is home to a mix of Bosniak, Serb, and Croat communities sharing a mountainous, landlocked landscape threaded with rivers. Sarajevo, the capital, famously hosted the 1984 Winter Olympics and is known for its blend of Ottoman, Austro-Hungarian, and socialist architectural heritage.
subTitle: 12 Cantons & Entities

history.label: Flag of Bosnia and Herzegovina
history.note: Imposed by High Representative Carlos Westendorp in 1998 after local politicians deadlocked, it was deliberately neutral. The yellow triangle suggests the country's shape and its three peoples; the stars represent Europe.

history.label: Republic of Bosnia (war-era)
history.note: A white flag with a blue shield of six golden fleurs-de-lis, echoing the medieval Kotromanić dynasty. It flew throughout the Bosnian War but was seen by Serbs and Croats as too Bosniak-centric.

history.label: SR Bosnia and Herzegovina
history.note: As a constituent Socialist Republic of Yugoslavia, Bosnia flew the plain Yugoslav red-white-blue tricolor with a gold-bordered red star — identical to the federal Yugoslav flag.

history.label: Austro-Hungarian Bosnia
history.note: After centuries under the Ottomans, Bosnia was occupied by Austria-Hungary in 1878 and formally annexed in 1908, flying a red-and-yellow flag with the provincial arms. Vienna's rule industrialised the province but stoked the Slav nationalism that, in Sarajevo in 1914, lit the fuse of the First World War.

history.label: Bosnian uprising (Husein Gradaščević)
history.note: In 1831 the Bosnian captain Husein Gradaščević led a revolt against Ottoman centralising reforms, briefly winning autonomy for Bosnia under a green flag with a gold crescent and star. The uprising was crushed in 1832, but "the Dragon of Bosnia" became an enduring folk hero of Bosnian self-rule.

predecessors.name: Austria-Hungary
predecessors.note: The civil ensign split 50/50, joining the Austrian red-white-red with Hungary's colors and both shields — the Dual Monarchy never agreed on a single flag for the whole empire.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Yugoslavia
predecessors.note: The blue-white-red Pan-Slavic tricolor symbolized the union of Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes, flown by the Kingdom until the Axis invasion of 1941.

predecessors.name: Socialist Yugoslavia
predecessors.note: Tito's Partisans added a red, gold-bordered star to the tricolor; it marked the country's communist character until the federation's violent breakup in the 1990s.

predecessors.name: Ottoman Empire
predecessors.note: The red field with white crescent and star was standardized in 1844; older Ottoman banners varied widely and often carried multiple crescents.

name: Bulgaria
overview: Bulgaria is a country in southeastern Europe on the Black Sea coast with one of the longest recorded national histories in Europe, having been established as a kingdom in 681 AD. The Cyrillic alphabet, now used by hundreds of millions of people, was developed by Bulgarian monks Saints Cyril and Methodius in the 9th century. Bulgaria is known for its rose oil production, ancient Thracian treasures, Black Sea resorts, and UNESCO-listed Rila Monastery.
subTitle: 28 Provinces

history.label: Current tricolor
history.note: With the fall of communism the socialist emblem was removed, returning Bulgaria to the plain white-green-red tricolor. The green was originally inspired by Russia's Pan-Slavic flag.

history.label: People's Republic (681 emblem)
history.note: The final socialist emblem added the date 681, marking the founding of the First Bulgarian Empire, alongside 1944 — the communist coup. This long-lived design ended in 1990.

history.label: People's Republic (revised emblem)
history.note: A redesigned emblem with white wheat replaced the earlier gold version; the lion-and-star device remained in the upper hoist canton.

history.label: People's Republic (first emblem)
history.note: The communist republic added a socialist emblem with a rampant lion, wheat ears and a red star to the canton, dated 9.IX.1944 for the communist coup.

history.label: Principality & Kingdom
history.note: After liberation from Ottoman rule, Bulgaria adopted the plain white-green-red tricolor, which served the principality and kingdom until the communist era — and was restored unchanged in 1990.

predecessors.name: Ottoman Empire
predecessors.note: The red field with white crescent and star was standardized in 1844; older Ottoman banners varied widely and often carried multiple crescents.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Bulgaria
predecessors.note: The white-green-red tricolor of the 'Third Bulgarian State', a kingdom under the Saxe-Coburg-Gotha tsars, abolished by referendum in favor of a communist republic in 1946.

predecessors.name: Eastern Rumelia
predecessors.note: An autonomous Ottoman province created by the Congress of Berlin, with a white flag and green canton; it unilaterally united with Bulgaria after a bloodless 1885 coup.

name: Croatia
overview: Croatia stretches along the eastern Adriatic Sea and is known for its dramatic coastline, more than a thousand islands, and well-preserved medieval cities. Dubrovnik, encircled by massive limestone walls, is one of Europe's most iconic historic cities and has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1979. Croatia joined the European Union in 2013 and is famous worldwide for the crystalline waterfalls of Plitvice Lakes National Park.
subTitle: 21 Counties

history.label: Flag of Croatia
history.note: Adopted in December 1990 as Yugoslavia dissolved, it centers the red-and-white checkerboard (šahovnica) beneath a crown of five historical regional shields, beginning with the oldest known Croatian coat of arms.

history.label: SR Croatia
history.note: The Socialist Republic placed a gold-bordered red star at the center of the tricolor, deliberately omitting the traditional checkerboard during the Yugoslav period.

history.label: Independent State of Croatia (NDH)
history.note: The WWII fascist puppet state kept the tricolor and checkerboard but added the Ustaše "U" emblem. It remains a taboo symbol because of the regime's genocidal crimes.

history.label: Banovina of Croatia
history.note: On the eve of WWII, Croatia won a measure of autonomy within the Kingdom of Yugoslavia as the Banovina (Banate) of Croatia in 1939, flying the plain red-white-blue tricolor with the checkerboard. This brief self-government was swept away in 1941 when the Axis invaded and dismembered Yugoslavia.

history.label: Labin Republic (Albona)
history.note: The Labin Republic was a short-lived 1921 workers' state in Istria, when coal miners seized the mines and declared self-rule against rising Italian Fascism. Its red flag bore a miner's hammer and pick. Italian troops crushed it within five weeks — one of Europe's earliest organized anti-fascist uprisings.

history.label: Kingdom of Croatia (Austria-Hungary)
history.note: Within Austria-Hungary, Croatia-Slavonia was an autonomous kingdom flying a red-white-blue tricolor with the historic checkerboard shield. The pan-Slavic red-white-blue arrangement, chosen in the 19th-century national revival, is the direct ancestor of the modern Croatian flag.

predecessors.name: Austria-Hungary
predecessors.note: The civil ensign split 50/50, joining the Austrian red-white-red with Hungary's colors and both shields — the Dual Monarchy never agreed on a single flag for the whole empire.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Yugoslavia
predecessors.note: The blue-white-red Pan-Slavic tricolor symbolized the union of Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes, flown by the Kingdom until the Axis invasion of 1941.

predecessors.name: Socialist Yugoslavia
predecessors.note: Tito's Partisans added a red, gold-bordered star to the tricolor; it marked the country's communist character until the federation's violent breakup in the 1990s.

predecessors.name: Italian Regency of Carnaro
predecessors.note: Poet Gabriele D'Annunzio's quasi-anarchic statelet at Fiume, whose flag bore the constellation of Ursa Major and the motto 'Quis contra nos?' — 'Who is against us?'.

predecessors.name: Free State of Fiume
predecessors.note: A short-lived independent city-state — modern Rijeka, Croatia — created by the Treaty of Rapallo and annexed by Italy in 1924. Its flag is a burgundy-yellow-blue tricolor.

predecessors.name: Republic of Ragusa
predecessors.note: The Adriatic merchant republic of Dubrovnik flew a white flag bearing St Blaise, its patron, holding a model of the walled city. Its motto was 'Libertas'.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Dalmatia
predecessors.note: A blue-and-yellow flag for the Habsburg crown land along the Adriatic coast, governed from Zadar, whose territory is now part of Croatia.

predecessors.name: Independent State of Croatia
predecessors.note: The Axis puppet state run by the fascist Ustaše, responsible for atrocities against Serbs, Jews, and Roma; its flag added the Ustaše's 'U' badge to the Croatian checkerboard.

name: Cyprus
overview: Cyprus is an island nation in the Eastern Mediterranean — the third-largest island in the sea — known as the mythological birthplace of the goddess Aphrodite. The island has been divided since 1974, with the northern portion under Turkish administration and the internationally recognised Republic of Cyprus governing the south. It is famous for its warm climate, ancient ruins, Venetian walls, and the unique copper-coloured map depicted on its national flag.
subTitle: 6 Districts

history.label: Copper island on white
history.note: In 2006 the shades were standardized and proportions set to 3:2. The map is rendered in copper for Cyprus's ancient copper mines, above two olive branches for peace — deliberately avoiding the colors of Greece and Turkey.

history.label: Independence flag (1960)
history.note: Adopted at independence from Britain in 1960, the flag deliberately avoided any cross or crescent and the blue/red of the two motherlands, aiming for neutrality between Greek and Turkish Cypriots.

history.label: British Crown Colony
history.note: A British protectorate from 1878 and a Crown Colony from 1925, Cyprus flew the British Blue Ensign defaced with the colonial badge — two red lions on a white shield, drawn from the arms of the Crusader Lusignan kings who once ruled the island. Cyprus had no flag of its own until independence in 1960.

name: Czechia
overview: The Czech Republic, also called Czechia, is a landlocked Central European country celebrated for its medieval architecture, rich brewing tradition, and wealth of historic towns. Prague, the capital, served as an imperial seat of the Holy Roman Empire and remains one of the best-preserved Gothic and Baroque cities in Europe. Czechoslovakia peacefully dissolved into the Czech Republic and Slovakia in 1993, an event known as the Velvet Divorce.
subTitle: 14 Regions

history.label: Czech flag (blue triangle)
history.note: Adopted by Czechoslovakia in 1920, adding a blue hoist triangle to distinguish it from Poland's. Czechia kept this exact flag after the peaceful 1993 split, despite a law barring successor states from using the federal flag.

history.label: Protectorate of Bohemia and Moravia
history.note: Under Nazi occupation (1939–1945) the Czech lands became the Protectorate of Bohemia and Moravia, flying a white-red-blue horizontal tricolor. The familiar blue-triangle national flag was suppressed for the duration and restored when Czechoslovakia was liberated in 1945.

history.label: White-red bicolour
history.note: Newly independent Czechoslovakia first flew the historic white-over-red bicolour of Bohemia — but it was nearly identical to Poland's flag, prompting the 1920 redesign.

predecessors.name: Austria-Hungary
predecessors.note: The civil ensign split 50/50, joining the Austrian red-white-red with Hungary's colors and both shields — the Dual Monarchy never agreed on a single flag for the whole empire.

predecessors.name: Czechoslovakia
predecessors.note: Adopted in 1920 with a blue hoist wedge to tell it apart from Poland's flag. Czechia kept it after the 1993 split, despite a clause meant to bar successor states from reusing it.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Bohemia
predecessors.note: The white-over-red bicolor of the Czech lands ruled from Prague; its kings were prince-electors of the Holy Roman Empire, and one, Charles IV, became Emperor himself.

predecessors.name: Margraviate of Moravia
predecessors.note: The eastern Czech land, a margraviate within the Bohemian crown; its yellow-and-red checkered eagle gives the modern Moravian flag its distinctive look.

name: Denmark
overview: Denmark is a Nordic nation comprising the Jutland Peninsula and numerous islands, consistently ranking among the world's happiest and most prosperous countries. The Danish flag, the Dannebrog, is the oldest national flag still in use by an independent nation, dating to at least 1370. Denmark is a constitutional monarchy with a long maritime trading tradition, and its territories include Greenland, the world's largest island.
subTitle: 5 Regions + 2 Territories

history.label: The Dannebrog
history.note: The Dannebrog — a white Nordic cross on red — is the oldest national flag still in continuous use by an independent nation, reliably documented from at least 1370. Legend holds it fell from the sky during the Battle of Lyndanisse in 1219. Whatever its origin, the design has remained essentially unchanged for over 650 years, making it the template for every other Nordic cross flag.

predecessors.name: Kalmar Union
predecessors.note: The dynastic union that joined Denmark, Sweden, and Norway under a single monarch for over a century, until Gustav Vasa led Sweden to break away in 1523.

name: Estonia
overview: Estonia is one of the three Baltic states on the eastern shore of the Baltic Sea, having re-declared independence from the Soviet Union in 1991 as one of its first republics to do so. It rapidly transformed into one of the world's most digitally advanced societies, pioneering e-government, digital voting, and the concept of e-residency. Tallinn, the capital, has one of the best-preserved medieval old towns in all of Northern Europe.
subTitle: 15 Counties

history.label: Flag of Estonia (restored)
history.note: The blue-black-white tricolour, first raised by Estonian students in 1884, was restored in 1990 as the USSR collapsed. Blue is the sky, black the soil and hardship, white the people's striving for freedom.

history.label: Estonian SSR
history.note: The Soviet-era flag placed a gold hammer, sickle, and star on red above blue wavy bands evoking the Baltic Sea.

history.label: Estonian SSR (early)
history.note: After Soviet annexation in 1940, a plain red flag with gold "ENSV" lettering and a hammer and sickle replaced the tricolour until the 1953 redesign.

history.label: Independence era
history.note: The same blue-black-white tricolour became the national flag at independence in 1918, before it was banned under Soviet rule and later restored.

predecessors.name: Soviet Union
predecessors.note: The gold hammer and sickle beneath a red star stood for the worker-peasant alliance. The exact shade and proportions were fixed by Soviet law in 1955.

predecessors.name: Estonian SSR
predecessors.note: A red flag with a wavy blue-and-white Baltic band at the base, the Soviet flag of Estonia, replaced when the blue-black-white national tricolor was restored in 1990.

name: Finland
overview: Finland is a Nordic country bordering Sweden, Norway, and Russia, known for its vast forests, tens of thousands of lakes, and the spectacular aurora borealis. It was part of Sweden for nearly 700 years before becoming a Russian Grand Duchy in 1809 and declaring independence in 1917. Finland consistently ranks at the very top of global happiness, education, and quality-of-life indices.
subTitle: 19 Regions

history.label: Finnish Flag (blue cross)
history.note: Adopted on May 29, 1918, shortly after Finland declared independence from Russia in December 1917. The blue Nordic cross on white was chosen through a design competition — the blue representing Finland's thousands of lakes and the clear sky, the white its snow. The exact proportions were refined several times, with the current form standardised in 1978.

history.label: Grand Duchy of Finland (Russian era)
history.note: After Sweden ceded Finland to Russia in 1809, Finland became an autonomous Grand Duchy of the Russian Empire. The duchy used a red flag bearing the Finnish lion coat of arms. During this period Finnish national identity grew steadily — the Kalevala epic was compiled, the Finnish language was promoted, and the eventual push for independence gathered strength.

history.label: Part of Sweden
history.note: For roughly 600 years — from the medieval Northern Crusades until 1809 — Finland was an integral eastern part of the Kingdom of Sweden, not a colony but Swedish soil, flying the blue-and-yellow Swedish flag. That long union left Finland its Lutheran faith, its legal traditions, and the Swedish-speaking minority that endures today, before Russia took it as war booty in 1809.

predecessors.name: Russian Empire
predecessors.note: The black-yellow-white 'imperial colors' were drawn from the state coat of arms by Alexander II, before Nicholas II demoted them for the white-blue-red tricolor.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Finland
predecessors.note: After independence Finland briefly elected a German prince as king and designed lion-bearing royal flags — but Germany's defeat in WWI ended the monarchy before he ever arrived.

name: France
overview: France is one of the world's most visited countries, attracting around 90 million tourists annually, and a founding member of the European Union and a permanent member of the UN Security Council. It has been a major cultural, philosophical, and political force in Western civilisation for centuries. Paris, the capital, is globally associated with art, fashion, gastronomy, the Eiffel Tower, and some of the world's greatest museums.
subTitle: 18 Regions

history.label: France (tricolore)
history.note: Louis-Philippe, the "citizen-king", restored the blue-white-red tricolore in 1830, and it has flown through every French republic and regime since. A slightly darker navy shade was quietly reintroduced in 2020.

history.label: Bourbon Restoration
history.note: When the Bourbons returned after Napoleon's fall, the tricolore and its revolutionary associations were dropped for the plain white royalist flag. The July Revolution of 1830 swept it away for good.

history.label: First Republic & Napoleon
history.note: In 1794 the tricolore was fixed as three equal vertical bands of blue, white, and red — the exact design still used today. It flew over the First Republic and Napoleon's First Empire.

history.label: Revolution (early tricolore)
history.note: The first revolutionary flag placed blue at the hoist and red at the fly — the reverse of the modern order — before the bands were swapped to their familiar arrangement in 1794.

history.label: Kingdom of France (Bourbon)
history.note: The white royal standard strewn with golden fleurs-de-lis was the emblem of the Ancien Régime monarchy. White was the dynastic color of the House of Bourbon until the Revolution.

history.label: Anglo-Corsican Kingdom
history.note: A brief Corsican kingdom under British protection during the French Revolutionary Wars.

history.label: Corsican Republic
history.note: Pasquale Paoli's enlightened Corsican republic — with one of the first democratic constitutions — before France bought and conquered the island.

history.label: Duchy of Lorraine
history.note: A long-independent duchy between France and the German lands, passed to France only in 1766.

history.label: Duchy of Brittany
history.note: An independent Celtic duchy with its own language and parliament, joined to the French crown by royal marriage in the 16th century.

history.label: Kingdom of Corsica
history.note: The improbable few-month kingdom of Theodor von Neuhoff, a German adventurer crowned king by Corsican rebels against Genoa.

history.label: Viscounty of Béarn
history.note: An independent Pyrenean state — the homeland of King Henry IV — that kept its own laws and parliament until merged into France.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of France
predecessors.note: The white field strewn with golden fleurs-de-lis was the banner of the Bourbon monarchy. White, not a tricolor, symbolized the king before the Revolution.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Corsica (1736)
predecessors.note: The white Moor's-head flag of the brief kingdom created when German adventurer Theodore von Neuhoff was crowned King of Corsica for a few months in 1736.

predecessors.name: Anglo-Corsican Kingdom
predecessors.note: Combined the British Union Jack with the Corsican Moor's head during the brief union under George III in the French Revolutionary Wars.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Bora Bora
predecessors.note: Red-and-white stripes with a canton of white stars, flown by the 19th-century Polynesian island kingdom before French annexation.

predecessors.name: County of Nice
predecessors.note: A red eagle perched on three green hills on white — the arms of Nice during its centuries under the House of Savoy, before France annexed it in 1860.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Raiatea
predecessors.note: A red-and-white striped flag with a small French tricolor canton, from the Leeward Islands kingdom that resisted French annexation for years.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Tubuai
predecessors.note: A white field with a red cross and four blue stars, the historic flag of a kingdom in the Austral Islands of the South Pacific.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Rapa Iti
predecessors.note: A remote Polynesian island kingdom whose flag carried a blue star beneath a crown with a French canton, before France absorbed it.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Mangareva
predecessors.note: The Gambier Islands kingdom flew a banded flag with corner stars, attributed to the French explorer Dumont d'Urville, before becoming a French protectorate.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Huahine
predecessors.note: A Society Islands kingdom that resisted France longer than its neighbours; its red-and-white flag with a French canton reflected an uneasy protectorate before annexation.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Tahiti
predecessors.note: The Pōmare dynasty's red-white-red flag flew over the Society Islands kingdom until France annexed Tahiti in 1880, ending nearly a century of independent Polynesian rule.

predecessors.name: Duchy of Burgundy
predecessors.note: The Valois dukes of Burgundy built one of the richest states in late-medieval Europe, rivaling the French crown, until Duke Charles the Bold fell at the Battle of Nancy in 1477.

predecessors.name: Duchy of Brittany
predecessors.note: The independent Breton duchy on France's Atlantic peninsula; its modern black-and-white 'Gwenn ha du' flag recalls the duchy absorbed into France through royal marriage in 1532.

predecessors.name: Duchy of Lorraine
predecessors.note: A gold flag with a red diagonal band and three white eaglets; the duchy on the German frontier passed to France only after its last duke became Holy Roman Emperor.

predecessors.name: Comtat Venaissin
predecessors.note: A papal enclave around Avignon ruled directly by the Popes for over five centuries — even surrounding the Avignon papacy — until France annexed it during the Revolution.

predecessors.name: County of Provence
predecessors.note: The gold-and-red striped banner of the medieval county, ruled by the House of Barcelona and then Anjou, before it was bequeathed to the French crown in 1481.

predecessors.name: County of Foix
predecessors.note: A Pyrenean county whose arms joined the gold-and-red pales of Foix with the cows of Béarn; its counts became kings of Navarre, and one, Henry, became King of France.

name: Germany
overview: Germany is the most populous country in the European Union and the largest economy in Europe, known worldwide for its engineering, automotive industry, and precision manufacturing. Reunified in 1990 after four decades of division, Germany became a leading force in European politics and global trade. Berlin, the capital, is one of Europe's most culturally vibrant cities with a history that spans Prussian grandeur, division, and remarkable reunification.
subTitle: 16 States

history.label: Germany (reunified)
history.note: On reunification in 1990 the West German black-red-gold tricolour became the flag of the whole country, with no change to the design. The colors are tied to the liberal revolutions of 1848.

history.label: West Germany (Federal Republic)
history.note: The Federal Republic readopted the Weimar black-red-gold tricolour in 1949. This plain flag won out as the flag of the reunified nation in 1990 and is still used today.

history.label: Nazi Germany
history.note: The 1935 Reich Flag Law made the swastika banner the sole national flag of Nazi Germany. A dark chapter of history, it was abolished by the Allies after the regime's defeat in 1945.

history.label: Nazi Germany (transitional)
history.note: After Hitler took power in 1933 the black-red-gold flag was banned, and for two years the old imperial black-white-red tricolour flew alongside the Nazi Party swastika as co-equal national flags.

history.label: Weimar Republic
history.note: The Weimar Constitution of 1919 adopted the black-red-gold tricolour, colors of the 1848 democratic movement. The choice was bitterly fought by conservatives who favored the old imperial colors.

history.label: German Empire
history.note: The black-white-red tricolour served the North German Confederation from 1867 and then the unified German Empire from 1871, until the monarchy collapsed at the end of World War I.

history.label: Duchy of Anhalt
history.note: The Duchy of Anhalt was a sovereign German state — ancestral home of the House of Ascania, from which Catherine the Great of Russia descended. Its green-white-red tricolor flew until the German monarchies fell in 1918. Anhalt is today part of Saxony-Anhalt.

history.label: Free City of Frankfurt
history.note: Before unification, Frankfurt was a sovereign city-state and seat of the German Confederation's parliament, flying a white-and-red civic flag until Prussia annexed it in 1866 for siding with Austria. It was one of the historic Free Cities, alongside Hamburg and Bremen.

history.label: Kingdom of Hanover
history.note: A north German kingdom in personal union with the British crown until 1837, annexed by Prussia after backing the losing side in 1866.

history.label: Kingdom of Westphalia
history.note: A short-lived Napoleonic client kingdom in western Germany, ruled by Napoleon's brother Jérôme Bonaparte as a 'model state'.

history.label: Kingdom of Bavaria
history.note: The second-largest kingdom of the German Empire, ruled from Munich by the Wittelsbachs, whose white-and-blue lozenges are still Bavaria's emblem.

history.label: Kingdom of Württemberg
history.note: A south-western German kingdom centred on Stuttgart, a constituent state of the German Empire until the 1918 revolution.

predecessors.name: German Empire
predecessors.note: The black-white-red tricolor combined Prussia's black-and-white with the Hanseatic red-and-white. It was officially adopted as the national flag only in 1892.

predecessors.name: Weimar Republic
predecessors.note: The black-red-gold tricolor revived the colors of the 1848 liberal revolution, deliberately tying the new democracy to earlier anti-autocratic movements.

predecessors.name: Nazi Germany
predecessors.note: The swastika flag became Germany's sole national flag in 1935. It was outlawed after WWII, and Germany returned to the black-red-gold of the Weimar era.

predecessors.name: East Germany (GDR)
predecessors.note: The hammer-and-compass-in-wheat emblem represented workers, intelligentsia, and farmers. Before 1959 East and West Germany flew the identical plain tricolor.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Prussia
predecessors.note: The black eagle on a white field derives from the Teutonic Knights. Prussia's black-and-white later supplied two of the three colors of the German Empire's flag.

predecessors.name: Holy Roman Empire
predecessors.note: The black double-headed eagle on gold was the Emperor's banner. Its two heads symbolized claimed authority over both the secular and the spiritual realms.

predecessors.name: Grand Duchy of Baden
predecessors.note: The red-over-yellow bicolor was Baden's flag from 1855. The southwestern grand duchy was one of the states that unified into the German Empire in 1871.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Hanover
predecessors.note: King Ernst August introduced the yellow-over-white bicolor in 1837 when Hanover's personal union with Britain ended. Its emblem was the white Saxon horse.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Bavaria
predecessors.note: The white-and-blue lozenge 'Rautenflagge' derives from the medieval arms of the Wittelsbachs. By rule it must show at least 21 lozenges with a white one in the top-right.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Westphalia
predecessors.note: This Napoleonic kingdom, ruled by Jérôme Bonaparte, flew a white-and-blue bicolor with crowned arms combining a Napoleonic eagle with the silver Saxon horse.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Saxony
predecessors.note: The white-over-green bicolor of the Wettin kings, who ruled from Dresden and joined the German Empire in 1871 while keeping their own monarch until 1918.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Württemberg
predecessors.note: Napoleon elevated the duchy of Württemberg to a kingdom in 1806; its black-over-red bicolor flew over Stuttgart until the monarchy fell at the end of World War I.

predecessors.name: Grand Duchy of Hesse
predecessors.note: A red-over-white bicolor for the Hesse-Darmstadt grand dukes, whose dynasty married into Europe's thrones — the last Tsarina of Russia was a Hessian princess.

predecessors.name: Electorate of Hesse
predecessors.note: The Hesse-Kassel state, the only German principality to keep the title 'Elector' after the Holy Roman Empire dissolved, until Prussia annexed it in 1866.

predecessors.name: Grand Duchies of Mecklenburg
predecessors.note: The blue-yellow-red tricolor shared by the two Mecklenburg grand duchies (Schwerin and Strelitz) on the Baltic coast, ruled by branches of the same Obotrite dynasty.

predecessors.name: Grand Duchy of Oldenburg
predecessors.note: A blue-red bicolor for the northwest German grand duchy whose ruling house also briefly held the thrones of Denmark, Greece, and Russia through dynastic marriage.

predecessors.name: Duchy of Brunswick
predecessors.note: The blue-over-yellow bicolor of the Welf dukes; its 'Black Duke' Frederick William raised a famous corps of black-clad hussars to fight Napoleon and died at Quatre Bras.

predecessors.name: Duchy of Nassau
predecessors.note: An orange-blue bicolor for the duchy on the Rhine and Lahn; its ruling house gave the Netherlands its royal dynasty, the House of Orange-Nassau.

predecessors.name: Grand Duchy of Saxe-Weimar-Eisenach
predecessors.note: The black-yellow-green flag of the Thuringian grand duchy whose Weimar court, under Goethe and Schiller, became the cultural heart of German classicism.

predecessors.name: Duchy of Saxe-Coburg and Gotha
predecessors.note: A green-white bicolor for the small Thuringian duchy that became the 'stud farm of Europe' — its princes married into the British, Belgian, Portuguese, and Bulgarian thrones.

predecessors.name: Principalities of Reuss
predecessors.note: The tiny Thuringian principalities famous for naming every male ruler Heinrich and numbering them in sequence — the elder line ran the count up to Heinrich LXVII.

predecessors.name: Principalities of Schwarzburg
predecessors.note: A blue-white bicolor for the two small Thuringian principalities of Rudolstadt and Sondershausen, ruled by one of Germany's oldest comital dynasties.

predecessors.name: Principality of Lippe
predecessors.note: A yellow-red bicolor for the small principality around Detmold, near the Teutoburg Forest where Germanic tribes annihilated three Roman legions in AD 9.

predecessors.name: Principality of Schaumburg-Lippe
predecessors.note: One of the smallest German states, a blue-red-white tricolor principality of barely 50,000 people that nonetheless survived as a sovereign state until 1918.

predecessors.name: Duchy of Anhalt
predecessors.note: A green-white bicolor for the central German duchy, ancestral home of the Ascanian dynasty and birthplace of Catherine the Great of Russia.

predecessors.name: Free City of Hamburg
predecessors.note: The white castle on red is the Hanseatic city-state's medieval emblem; its three towers and the cross-topped gate symbolize the free port's civic independence.

predecessors.name: Free City of Bremen
predecessors.note: The red-and-white 'Speckflagge' (bacon flag) of checkered stripes flew over the Hanseatic port-republic, one of the founding member states of the German Empire.

predecessors.name: Free City of Lübeck
predecessors.note: The white-over-red bicolor of the 'Queen of the Hanseatic League', the city that led the medieval Baltic trade network before the Nazis stripped its statehood in 1937.

predecessors.name: Free City of Frankfurt
predecessors.note: The white-over-red city republic that hosted the German Confederation's parliament, until Prussia annexed it for siding against it in the 1866 war.

predecessors.name: German Confederation
predecessors.note: The loose league of German states formed after Napoleon's defeat flew a black-red-gold war flag — the same liberal colors later adopted by unified Germany.

predecessors.name: Saar Protectorate
predecessors.note: A French-administered, semi-autonomous state after WWII, its flag a Nordic cross in French blue and red. A 1955 referendum returned the coal-rich Saar to West Germany.

predecessors.name: Grand Duchy of Berg
predecessors.note: A Napoleonic client state on the Rhine, first ruled by Marshal Murat; its white flag bore a red lion before it was dissolved after Napoleon's defeat.

name: Greece
overview: Greece is widely regarded as the cradle of Western civilisation, birthplace of democracy, Western philosophy, the Olympic Games, and significant foundations of modern literature and science. The country consists of a mountainous mainland peninsula and more than 6,000 islands, of which about 230 are permanently inhabited. Athens, one of the world's oldest cities, has been continuously inhabited for at least 7,000 years and is home to the ancient Acropolis.
subTitle: 13 Regions

history.label: Nine-stripe flag
history.note: Adopted as the sole national flag in 1978, with nine alternating blue and white stripes and a white cross in the canton. Tradition links the nine stripes to the syllables of "Eleftheria i Thanatos" — "Freedom or Death".

history.label: Junta cross flag
history.note: During the military junta the plain cross land flag was rendered in a very dark navy blue and made the sole national flag in 1970. It was abolished after the restoration of democracy.

history.label: Blue land flag with white cross
history.note: Standardized by the Provisional Government in 1822 as the "land flag" — a plain blue field with a centered white cross. It served on and off as the national land flag for over 150 years.

history.label: Revolution cross flag
history.note: A blue flag with a white cross used by Greek revolutionaries, first appearing in the 1769 Orlov Revolt and becoming the most widespread banner of the 1821 War of Independence. The cross symbolized the Orthodox faith that unified the fighters.

predecessors.name: Byzantine Empire
predecessors.note: The four gold firesteels (often glossed 'King of Kings, Ruling over Kings') were the emblem of the Palaiologos dynasty, the last imperial house, which fell with Constantinople in 1453.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Greece
predecessors.note: The plain blue field with a white cross was the official land flag from 1822. The blue-and-white striped version initially served only as the naval ensign.

predecessors.name: Cretan State
predecessors.note: Autonomous Crete under Prince George of Greece; its blue flag with a white cross adds a red canton with a white star, symbolizing remaining Ottoman suzerainty.

predecessors.name: Septinsular Republic
predecessors.note: A blue field with the golden Lion of St Mark holding seven arrows for the seven Ionian islands — the first autonomous Greek state of the modern era.

predecessors.name: Principality of Pindus
predecessors.note: A green-white-blue flag tied to a WWII Axis-sponsored Aromanian (Vlach) state in the Pindus mountains, whose actual existence is disputed by historians.

predecessors.name: Principality of Samos
predecessors.note: A cross flag with two red and two blue quarters, the red marking Ottoman suzerainty over this autonomous Greek island principality, which united with Greece in 1912.

predecessors.name: Duchy of the Archipelago
predecessors.note: A Venetian crusader state ruling the Cyclades from Naxos, under the Sanudo dynasty whose white banner bore a red cross and four black crosslets.

predecessors.name: Republic of Pontus
predecessors.note: A proposed homeland for the Pontic Greeks of the Black Sea coast, never realized; its black flag bore the eagle of the medieval Empire of Trebizond.

predecessors.name: United States of the Ionian Islands
predecessors.note: A British protectorate over the Ionian Islands, flying the Lion of St Mark on a Union-Jack-cantoned flag, until the islands were ceded to Greece in 1864.

name: Hungary
overview: Hungary is a landlocked Central European country with a language unrelated to its Slavic and Germanic neighbours, belonging to the Finno-Ugric language family. Budapest, formed by the union of Buda and Pest in 1873, is often called the 'Paris of the East' for its grand boulevards, thermal baths, and ornate parliament building on the Danube. Hungary was a major power within the Austro-Hungarian Empire and joined the European Union in 2004.
subTitle: 20 Counties

history.label: Current tricolor
history.note: After the 1956 revolution was crushed, the Kádár government removed all heraldry, returning to the plain red-white-green tricolor. This emblem-free flag has remained Hungary's flag ever since.

history.label: 1956 Revolution 'flag with the hole'
history.note: Revolutionaries cut the hated communist coat of arms out of the flag's center, leaving an iconic ragged hole. This "hole flag" became the enduring emblem of the 1956 uprising against Soviet rule.

history.label: Communist (Rákosi arms)
history.note: The Stalinist Rákosi regime placed a Soviet-style emblem of wheat, hammer and red star at the flag's center — a foreign-looking symbol that Hungarians widely resented.

history.label: Republican (Kossuth arms)
history.note: The post-war Second Hungarian Republic restored the small Kossuth coat of arms to the tricolor. The same flag returned briefly during the 1956 revolution.

history.label: Pre-communist tricolor
history.note: The plain red-white-green tricolor flew over the interwar Kingdom of Hungary — a kingdom with no king, ruled by Regent Horthy.

history.label: Kingdom of Hungary (Austria-Hungary)
history.note: Within the Austro-Hungarian Empire, the Kingdom of Hungary flew the red-white-green tricolor charged with the crowned middle coat of arms, often flanked by angels. It was the flag of the Hungarian half of the Dual Monarchy, at the height of Budapest's belle-époque grandeur, until the empire collapsed in 1918.

history.label: Árpád stripes (medieval kingdom)
history.note: The red-and-white horizontal stripes (the "Árpád stripes") were the banner of the House of Árpád, Hungary's founding dynasty, from around the 11th century. One of the oldest heraldic devices in Europe, they still occupy the left half of Hungary's coat of arms and remain a powerful — and politically contested — national symbol.

predecessors.name: Austria-Hungary
predecessors.note: The civil ensign split 50/50, joining the Austrian red-white-red with Hungary's colors and both shields — the Dual Monarchy never agreed on a single flag for the whole empire.

predecessors.name: Hungarian Soviet Republic
predecessors.note: Béla Kun's plain red communist state, the second Soviet republic in the world after Russia, which lasted just 133 days before Romanian troops toppled it.

predecessors.name: Hungarian People's Republic
predecessors.note: The communist 'Rákosi coat of arms' on the tricolor was so hated that 1956 revolutionaries cut it out, leaving a hole-in-the-flag that became the uprising's emblem.

name: Iceland
overview: Iceland is a Nordic island nation in the North Atlantic, the most sparsely populated country in Europe and one of the most geologically active places on Earth, home to active volcanoes, geysers, hot springs, and vast lava fields. Despite its position near the Arctic Circle, the Gulf Stream keeps its coastal areas relatively mild. Iceland's parliament, the Althing, founded in 930 AD, is the world's oldest parliament still in operation.
subTitle: 8 Regions

history.label: Current Icelandic flag
history.note: The red-cross-on-white-on-blue design was retained when Iceland became a fully independent republic in 1944. The colors are said to represent the island's fire (red), ice (white) and mountains (blue).

history.label: Sovereign era (Danish crown)
history.note: A red cross was added inside the white cross by royal decree in 1915, and this became Iceland's flag when it gained sovereignty under the Danish crown in 1918 — the same design as today.

history.label: Hvítbláinn (white-blue)
history.note: Before the modern flag, Icelandic nationalists rallied behind the Hvítbláinn — a white cross on a sky-blue field, designed in 1897. Denmark refused to recognise it because it resembled the flag of Greece, and the dispute pushed Iceland to add the red cross, producing the blue-white-red design adopted in 1915.

name: Ireland
overview: Ireland is an island nation in northwestern Europe known for its lush green landscapes, rich literary heritage from writers like Oscar Wilde and James Joyce, and vibrant Celtic culture. The island is divided between the Republic of Ireland and Northern Ireland, which is part of the United Kingdom. Ireland joined the European Economic Community in 1973 and has since become one of Europe's most prosperous economies, home to the European headquarters of many global technology companies.
subTitle: 4 Provinces · 26 Counties

history.label: Green-white-orange tricolour
history.note: First flown by Thomas Francis Meagher in 1848 and raised over the GPO during the 1916 Rising, the tricolour was used by the Irish Free State from 1922 and given constitutional status in 1937. Green represents Catholics, orange Protestants, and the white the hoped-for peace between them.

history.label: Green flag with gold harp
history.note: A green field bearing a golden harp was recognized as the flag of Ireland from the mid-1600s and remained the dominant nationalist emblem right up to the 1916 Easter Rising. The same design survives today as the flag of Leinster.

predecessors.name: Irish Republic (1916)
predecessors.note: The green flag with gold lettering 'Irish Republic' flown over Dublin's General Post Office during the Easter Rising. The original captured banner survives in Dublin.

predecessors.name: Confederate Ireland
predecessors.note: The Catholic Confederation based at Kilkenny flew a green field with a gold harp and a white canton bearing a red cross during the Eleven Years' War.

predecessors.name: Lordship of Ireland
predecessors.note: Three gold crowns on light blue with a white border, the banner of the medieval English lordship before the gold-harp arms replaced it under Henry VIII.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Ireland
predecessors.note: The red saltire of St Patrick on white represented the kingdom under the English (later British) crown; it survives as the Irish element of the modern Union Flag.

name: Italy
overview: Italy is a southern European country occupying the boot-shaped peninsula that reaches into the Mediterranean, together with the islands of Sicily and Sardinia. The heart of the ancient Roman Empire and the birthplace of the Renaissance, Italy has shaped Western art, architecture, music, and cuisine more than almost any other nation. It holds more UNESCO World Heritage Sites than any country on Earth, and Rome, the capital, completely surrounds the independent Vatican City. Italy unified into a single kingdom only in 1861.
subTitle: 20 Regions

history.label: Italian Republic
history.note: After the 1946 referendum abolished the monarchy, the Savoy royal arms were removed, leaving the plain green-white-red tricolore enshrined in the 1948 Constitution. It remains Italy's flag today.

history.label: Kingdom of Italy
history.note: On unification in 1861 the tricolore carried the coat of arms of the House of Savoy at its centre. This flag flew for 85 years through both World Wars until the monarchy was abolished.

history.label: Republic of San Marco (Venice)
history.note: During the 1848 revolutions, Venice rose against Austrian rule and proclaimed the Republic of San Marco under Daniele Manin, placing the gold Winged Lion of Saint Mark on the Italian tricolor. Austria besieged and retook the city in 1849 after seventeen months — one of the last holdouts of the revolutionary wave.

history.label: Napoleonic Kingdom of Italy
history.note: A striking design of a red field bearing a white lozenge enclosing a green square. Adopted by Napoleon's Italian Republic in 1802, it carried over when he made himself King of Italy in 1805.

history.label: Cispadane Republic (first tricolore)
history.note: On 7 January 1797 in Reggio Emilia, the Cispadane Republic adopted a green-white-red tricolour — the very first official use of the Italian tricolore as a national flag.

history.label: Duchy of Amalfi
history.note: Amalfi was one of the first Italian maritime republics, a Mediterranean trading power from the 9th to 12th centuries that rivalled Venice, Pisa, and Genoa. Its banner bore a white cross on blue — later associated with the Order of Malta. Norman conquest in 1131 ended its independence.

history.label: Kingdom of the Two Sicilies
history.note: The largest of the pre-unification Italian states — Naples and Sicily under the Bourbons — conquered by Garibaldi's Redshirts and folded into a united Italy.

predecessors.name: Republic of Venice
predecessors.note: The golden winged Lion of St Mark holds an open Gospel; by tradition the book was shown open in peacetime and the lion grasped a sword in war.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of the Two Sicilies
predecessors.note: The white Bourbon field carries an elaborate coat of arms of the many southern Italian domains the dynasty ruled, until Garibaldi's forces conquered it in 1860–61.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Sardinia
predecessors.note: A white field with the red Savoy cross in a blue border. After 1848 Sardinia switched to the tricolor with the Savoy shield, which became the basis of the Kingdom of Italy's flag.

predecessors.name: Republic of Genoa
predecessors.note: The red cross of St George on white was Genoa's medieval ensign. By one account England paid Genoa in 1190 for the right to fly it for naval protection.

predecessors.name: Cisalpine Republic
predecessors.note: This Napoleonic sister republic in northern Italy flew a vertical green-white-red tricolor modeled on revolutionary France — a direct forerunner of the modern Italian flag.

predecessors.name: Republic of Florence
predecessors.note: The red giglio — a stylized lily — on white was the emblem of the medieval commune. Florence reversed the earlier white-on-red Ghibelline lily to mark its Guelph allegiance.

predecessors.name: Republic of Siena
predecessors.note: The black-and-white Balzana is said to recall the white and black smoke that rose when Siena's legendary founders Senius and Aschius made sacrifices on the city's hills.

predecessors.name: Republic of Noli
predecessors.note: One of the smallest and least-known Italian maritime republics, this tiny Ligurian town governed itself for six centuries under the protection of allied Genoa.

predecessors.name: Duchy of Savoy
predecessors.note: The white cross on red of the House of Savoy, the dynasty that ruled an Alpine state straddling France and Italy and eventually became the royal house of a united Italy.

predecessors.name: Duchy of Modena and Reggio
predecessors.note: Ruled by the Este dynasty and later the Austrian Habsburg-Este line, the duchy flew a tricolour charged with the Este eagle until it was absorbed into unified Italy.

predecessors.name: Duchy of Mantua
predecessors.note: The Gonzaga dynasty's duchy in the Po plain, a great Renaissance art and music centre, flew a flag quartered with their eagles and lions before passing to Austria.

predecessors.name: Duchy of Ferrara
predecessors.note: The Este capital and a brilliant Renaissance court; when the last legitimate Este duke died without heirs in 1597, the Pope reclaimed Ferrara for the Papal States.

predecessors.name: Duchy of Urbino
predecessors.note: The Montefeltro and later Della Rovere dukes made Urbino one of the foremost humanist courts of the Renaissance, birthplace of the painter Raphael.

predecessors.name: Marquisate of Montferrat
predecessors.note: A long-lived northern Italian march whose Aleramici marquises led crusades and even briefly held the throne of the Kingdom of Thessalonica.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Naples
predecessors.note: The independent southern kingdom before its 1816 merger with Sicily; its white flag bore the arms of the successive Aragonese, Spanish, and Bourbon dynasties that ruled it.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Naples (Napoleonic)
predecessors.note: Under Napoleon's marshal Joachim Murat, Naples flew a white field with a red border and a central emblem, before Murat was deposed and executed in 1815.

predecessors.name: Transpadane Republic
predecessors.note: A short-lived Napoleonic sister republic around Milan that flew a vertical green-white-red tricolour, soon merged with the Cispadane Republic to form the Cisalpine Republic.

predecessors.name: Roman Republic (1798)
predecessors.note: A Napoleonic republic proclaimed after French troops seized Rome and deposed Pope Pius VI; its black-white-red tricolour flew until Neapolitan and Austrian forces restored papal rule.

predecessors.name: Partisan Republic of Ossola
predecessors.note: A free zone liberated by anti-Fascist partisans in the Alps near the Swiss border, self-governing for forty days in 1944 before German and Fascist forces retook it.

predecessors.name: Partisan Republic of Alba
predecessors.note: A Piedmontese town held free by partisans for 23 days in 1944, immortalized in Beppe Fenoglio's novel 'I ventitré giorni della città di Alba'.

predecessors.name: Italian Social Republic
predecessors.note: Mussolini's German-backed rump state in northern Italy, often called the Republic of Salò; its war flag added a fasces-clutching eagle to the tricolour.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Italy
predecessors.note: The green-white-red tricolor charged with the crowned shield of the House of Savoy, flown through both World Wars until the 1946 referendum abolished the monarchy.

predecessors.name: Grand Duchy of Tuscany
predecessors.note: The red-white-red triband charged with the grand-ducal crown was used by the Habsburg-Lorraine grand dukes who ruled Florence and Tuscany.

predecessors.name: Republic of San Marco
predecessors.note: Daniele Manin's revolutionary Venetian republic flew the Italian tricolor with the winged Lion of St Mark in the canton, until Austria retook Venice in 1849.

predecessors.name: Republic of Cospaia
predecessors.note: A tiny accidental republic on the Tuscan–Papal border, created by a surveying error and famous for freely growing tobacco. Its flag is divided black and white diagonally.

predecessors.name: Anconine Republic
predecessors.note: A Napoleonic sister republic around the Adriatic port of Ancona, flying a light blue–yellow–red tricolor with the republic's name lettered across it.

predecessors.name: Parthenopean Republic
predecessors.note: A short-lived Neapolitan sister republic that joined French blue with the yellow and red of Naples, crushed within months by a royalist peasant army.

predecessors.name: Cispadane Republic
predecessors.note: The first Italian horizontal red-white-green tricolor, with a central quiver of four arrows. Adopted on 7 January 1797, it is commemorated as Italy's Tricolour Day.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Tavolara
predecessors.note: A tiny island kingdom off Sardinia ruled by the Bertoleoni family, sometimes called the smallest kingdom in the world. Its flag bears a gold star beneath a crown.

predecessors.name: Principality of Elba
predecessors.note: The tiny Mediterranean island Napoleon was given to rule during his first exile; he designed its white flag with a red bar and three gold bees before escaping to retake France.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Etruria
predecessors.note: A short-lived Napoleonic client kingdom in Tuscany under the Bourbon-Parma dynasty, before Napoleon annexed it directly into France.

predecessors.name: Duchy of Parma
predecessors.note: A northern Italian duchy long ruled by the Farnese and later Bourbon-Parma dynasties, its yellow flag bearing a blue fleur-de-lis shield, absorbed into unified Italy.

predecessors.name: Principality of Lucca and Piombino
predecessors.note: A Napoleonic state given to the emperor's sister Elisa Bonaparte, the only one of his siblings to rule in her own right.

name: Kosovo
overview: Kosovo is one of the world's newest countries, having declared independence from Serbia in 2008 following a period of international administration after the 1998-99 conflict. It is recognised by over 100 UN member states and is a predominantly Albanian-populated territory with significant Serbian Orthodox cultural heritage, including medieval monasteries that are UNESCO World Heritage Sites. Pristina is its rapidly developing capital and largest city.
subTitle: 7 Districts

history.label: Flag of Kosovo
history.note: Adopted on 17 February 2008 with the declaration of independence — one of the world's newest national flags. It deliberately avoids Albanian and pan-Slavic colors, using a blue field with a gold map and six white stars for the main ethnic groups.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Yugoslavia
predecessors.note: The blue-white-red Pan-Slavic tricolor symbolized the union of Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes, flown by the Kingdom until the Axis invasion of 1941.

predecessors.name: Socialist Yugoslavia
predecessors.note: Tito's Partisans added a red, gold-bordered star to the tricolor; it marked the country's communist character until the federation's violent breakup in the 1990s.

name: Latvia
overview: Latvia is one of the three Baltic states, situated between Estonia and Lithuania on the eastern shore of the Baltic Sea. It regained independence from the Soviet Union in 1991 and joined both NATO and the European Union in 2004. Riga, the capital, is the largest city in the Baltic states and is renowned for its outstanding collection of Art Nouveau architecture — the largest concentration of such buildings in the world.
subTitle: 6 Regions

history.label: Flag of Latvia (restored)
history.note: Among the world's oldest flag designs, traced to a 13th-century chronicle, the maroon-white-maroon flag was restored in 1990. Its deep carmine shade is officially called "Latvian red".

history.label: Latvian SSR
history.note: A gold hammer, sickle, and star on red above white and blue wavy bands symbolizing the sea.

history.label: Latvian SSR (early)
history.note: Imposed after the 1940 Soviet annexation, a red flag with gold "Latvijas PSR" lettering and a hammer and sickle, used until the 1953 redesign.

history.label: Independence era
history.note: The ancient carmine-and-white flag was adopted at independence in 1918 before Soviet annexation, then restored unchanged in 1990.

predecessors.name: Soviet Union
predecessors.note: The gold hammer and sickle beneath a red star stood for the worker-peasant alliance. The exact shade and proportions were fixed by Soviet law in 1955.

predecessors.name: Duchy of Courland and Semigallia
predecessors.note: A Polish-Lithuanian vassal duchy in modern Latvia that — astonishingly — founded colonies on Tobago in the Caribbean and an island in the Gambia in the 1650s.

predecessors.name: Latvian SSR
predecessors.note: A red flag whose lower edge bore stylized white waves of the Baltic Sea beneath the hammer, sickle and star, used until Latvia restored its independence.

name: Liechtenstein
overview: Liechtenstein is a tiny doubly landlocked microstate in Central Europe nestled between Switzerland and Austria, and is one of only two countries in the world entirely surrounded by landlocked countries. Despite covering just 160 square kilometres, it has one of the world's highest per-capita incomes and is a significant international financial centre. Liechtenstein is a constitutional monarchy ruled by the Prince of Liechtenstein and has no airport.
subTitle: 11 Communes

history.label: Blue-red with crown
history.note: A restyled, more detailed gold crown was adopted in 1982. Blue represents the sky and red the evening fires lit in homes.

history.label: Crown added (1937)
history.note: At the 1936 Berlin Olympics, Liechtenstein discovered its flag was identical to Haiti's. To tell them apart, it added a gold princely crown in 1937 — also signifying its status as a principality.

history.label: Blue-red (no crown)
history.note: The plain blue-over-red bicolour was enshrined in the 1921 constitution — the very flag whose clash with Haiti's prompted the crown to be added.

name: Lithuania
overview: Lithuania is the southernmost and largest of the three Baltic states and was historically the centre of the Grand Duchy of Lithuania, one of the largest states in medieval Europe. It was the first Soviet republic to declare independence in 1990 and joined both NATO and the EU in 2004. Vilnius, the capital, is known for its remarkably well-preserved Baroque old town, a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
subTitle: 10 Counties

history.label: Flag of Lithuania (restored)
history.note: Yellow for the sun and prosperity, green for forests and hope, red for blood shed for the nation. It was the first Baltic flag readopted during the independence movement, restored in 1988.

history.label: Lithuanian SSR
history.note: A gold hammer, sickle, and star on a red field above white and green horizontal bands.

history.label: Lithuanian SSR (early)
history.note: After the 1940 Soviet occupation, a plain red flag with gold "LIETUVOS TSR" lettering and a hammer and sickle, used until the 1953 redesign.

history.label: Independence era
history.note: The yellow-green-red tricolour was adopted at independence in 1918, banned under Soviet rule, and restored identical in 1988.

history.label: Grand Duchy of Lithuania (Vytis)
history.note: For centuries Lithuania was not a small Baltic state but one of medieval Europe's largest countries — the Grand Duchy of Lithuania, stretching from the Baltic nearly to the Black Sea. Its banner was the Vytis: a white armoured knight charging on horseback against a red field. After union with Poland it shared in the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth until the partitions erased both in 1795. The red Vytis flag survives today as Lithuania's official historical state flag.

predecessors.name: Soviet Union
predecessors.note: The gold hammer and sickle beneath a red star stood for the worker-peasant alliance. The exact shade and proportions were fixed by Soviet law in 1955.

predecessors.name: Republic of Central Lithuania
predecessors.note: A Polish client state around Vilnius; its red field bore the Polish White Eagle and the Lithuanian Vytis knight side by side, before annexation by Poland in 1922.

predecessors.name: Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth
predecessors.note: One of the largest states in Europe, an elective monarchy with an early constitution and a 'noble democracy', partitioned out of existence by Russia, Prussia, and Austria.

predecessors.name: Lithuanian SSR
predecessors.note: A red flag with white and green lower bands, flown over Soviet Lithuania until the republic became the first to declare independence from the USSR in 1990.

predecessors.name: Grand Duchy of Lithuania
predecessors.note: The red banner with the white Vytis — an armored knight on a charging horse — flew over a realm that at its height stretched from the Baltic to the Black Sea.

predecessors.name: Memel Territory
predecessors.note: A League of Nations territory around the Baltic port of Klaipėda, seized by Lithuania in 1923; Nazi Germany forced its cession in 1939, one of the last crises before WWII.

name: Luxembourg
overview: Luxembourg is a small landlocked grand duchy in Western Europe, bordered by Belgium, France, and Germany, and is one of the wealthiest countries in the world per capita. It hosts key European Union institutions and is a major centre for European banking and finance. Despite its tiny size, Luxembourg has three official languages — Luxembourgish, French, and German — reflecting its position at the crossroads of European cultures.
subTitle: 12 Cantons

history.label: Red-white-blue tricolor
history.note: Fixed in 1845 and only legally formalized in 1993, Luxembourg's tricolor has been essentially unchanged for over 175 years. It is nearly identical to the Netherlands' flag but is longer and uses a lighter blue.

history.label: Roude Léiw (the Red Lion)
history.note: Long before the tricolor, Luxembourg's emblem was the "Roude Léiw" — a red, double-tailed, crowned lion on a field of white and blue stripes, the arms of the medieval Counts (later Dukes) of Luxembourg dating to the 13th century. It still flies today as the country's civil ensign and is cherished as the older, more distinctly Luxembourgish banner.

name: Malta
overview: Malta is a small Mediterranean archipelago south of Sicily and one of the world's smallest and most densely populated countries. Its strategic location has made it a crossroads of civilisations for millennia, ruled by the Phoenicians, Romans, Arabs, Normans, Knights of St John, French, and British. The island has an extraordinary concentration of prehistoric temples, Baroque architecture, and ancient fortifications, and joined the EU in 2004.
subTitle: 5 Regions

history.label: White-red with George Cross
history.note: At independence in 1964 the blue canton was dropped and the George Cross, edged in red, was placed directly on the white field so it would appear less prominent.

history.label: George Cross in a blue canton
history.note: The George Cross, awarded to the Maltese people by King George VI in 1942 for their WWII bravery during the Siege of Malta, was added to the flag in 1943 on a blue canton.

history.label: British Malta
history.note: Malta became a British colony in 1813 and a vital naval fortress guarding the Mediterranean, flying the Union Jack (and a colonial blue ensign at sea) until the George Cross was added in 1943. Its strategic harbour made it one of the most heavily bombed places on earth during WWII's Siege of Malta.

history.label: Order of Saint John (Knights of Malta)
history.note: For over 250 years Malta was ruled by the Knights Hospitaller — the Sovereign Military Order of Saint John — who flew a plain white cross on red. Granted the islands in 1530, the Knights famously withstood the Great Siege of 1565 by the Ottomans before Napoleon expelled them in 1798. Their eight-pointed white cross endures as the "Maltese cross."

predecessors.name: Sovereign Military Order of Malta
predecessors.note: The white eight-pointed Maltese cross on red is the Knights Hospitaller's 'works' flag; the eight points are traditionally said to represent the eight Beatitudes.

name: Moldova
overview: Moldova is a small landlocked country in Eastern Europe bordered by Romania and Ukraine, one of Europe's least visited yet historically fascinating nations. Gaining independence from the Soviet Union in 1991, it navigates the challenge of the breakaway region of Transnistria, which has functioned as a de facto separate state since 1990. Moldova has a long winemaking tradition stretching back thousands of years and is known for producing excellent wines.
subTitle: 35 Districts

history.label: Flag of Moldova
history.note: A blue-yellow-red vertical tricolour adopted in 1990, deliberately modelled on Romania's flag to reflect shared heritage, but bearing Moldova's eagle-and-aurochs coat of arms on the central stripe.

history.label: Moldavian SSR
history.note: A red field with a central green stripe and a gold hammer, sickle, and star. Its red-green-red layout closely resembles the flag of present-day breakaway Transnistria.

history.label: Part of Romania (Bessarabia)
history.note: Most of present-day Moldova — historic Bessarabia — united with Romania in 1918 after a century under the Russian Empire, flying the Romanian flag. The 1939 Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact handed it to the USSR in 1940, which carved out the Moldavian SSR — the root of Moldova's lasting question of identity between Romania and the post-Soviet world.

history.label: Principality of Moldavia
history.note: The medieval Principality of Moldavia — the realm of Stephen the Great, who held off Ottoman, Hungarian and Polish armies — flew a red banner bearing the head of an aurochs (a wild ox), the enduring symbol of the Moldavian lands. In 1859 Moldavia united with Wallachia to form modern Romania; the eastern half later became Moldova.

predecessors.name: Soviet Union
predecessors.note: The gold hammer and sickle beneath a red star stood for the worker-peasant alliance. The exact shade and proportions were fixed by Soviet law in 1955.

predecessors.name: Moldavian SSR
predecessors.note: A red flag with a central green stripe and the hammer, sickle and star, flown by Soviet Moldova before the country adopted a Romanian-style tricolor in 1990.

name: Monaco
overview: Monaco is a tiny city-state on the French Riviera, the world's second-smallest country by area after Vatican City, and the most densely populated sovereign state in the world. Synonymous with luxury, it is home to the famous Monte Carlo Casino, the Formula One Monaco Grand Prix, and a high concentration of the world's wealthiest individuals. The Grimaldi family has ruled Monaco since 1297, making it one of the world's oldest ruling dynasties.
subTitle: 4 Quarters

history.label: Red-white bicolour
history.note: Adopted in 1881 from the Grimaldi heraldic colors, this simple red-over-white bicolour is among the world's oldest continuous national flags. It is nearly identical to Indonesia's flag, differing mainly in proportion.

history.label: Armorial flag (Grimaldi arms)
history.note: Before the modern bicolour, Monaco flew a white flag bearing the Grimaldi dynasty's red-and-white lozenge coat of arms with crown and supporters.

history.label: Free Cities of Menton and Roquebrune
history.note: Two Riviera towns that broke away from Monaco in 1848 and were eventually sold to France, shrinking the principality to its present tiny size.

predecessors.name: Monaco (Grimaldi lozenge banner)
predecessors.note: The Grimaldi armorial banner of red-and-white lozenges, the dynasty's historic flag before Monaco adopted its plain red-over-white bicolour in 1881.

name: Montenegro
overview: Montenegro is a small Balkan nation on the Adriatic Sea that declared independence from the State Union of Serbia and Montenegro in 2006. Its name means 'Black Mountain' in Italian, referring to the imposing dark peak of Mount Lovćen. The country is known for its dramatic mountain landscape, medieval walled towns, and the stunning Bay of Kotor, sometimes described as Europe's southernmost fjord.
subTitle: 25 Municipalities

history.label: Flag of Montenegro
history.note: Adopted in 2004 ahead of independence: a red field with a gold border bearing the golden double-headed eagle of the historic House of Petrović-Njegoš, a deliberate break from the Serbian-style tricolor.

history.label: Plain tricolor
history.note: A red, light-blue and white tricolor in a 1:3 ratio — then the longest national flag proportions in the world — and nearly indistinguishable from Serbia's plain tricolor of the same era.

history.label: SR Montenegro
history.note: The Socialist Republic's red-blue-white tricolor with a centered gold-bordered red star was visually identical to SR Serbia's flag.

history.label: Kingdom of Montenegro
history.note: Montenegro was an independent principality and then, from 1910, a kingdom under Nicholas I — the last of the warrior prince-bishops' line — flying a red-blue-white tricolor charged with the royal cypher and the double-headed eagle.
