Tiny but fiercely independent, the kingdom vanished in 1918 when it was absorbed into the new Yugoslavia, a loss that fuelled Montenegrin identity debates for a century.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Yugoslavia
predecessors.note: The blue-white-red Pan-Slavic tricolor symbolized the union of Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes, flown by the Kingdom until the Axis invasion of 1941.

predecessors.name: Socialist Yugoslavia
predecessors.note: Tito's Partisans added a red, gold-bordered star to the tricolor; it marked the country's communist character until the federation's violent breakup in the 1990s.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Montenegro
predecessors.note: The red flag bearing the crowned cypher of King Nikola I, ruler of the small Balkan mountain kingdom that merged into Yugoslavia after World War I.

name: Netherlands
overview: The Netherlands is a densely populated country in Western Europe famous for its flat landscape, extensive canal systems, windmills, tulip fields, and masters of Dutch Golden Age painting such as Rembrandt and Vermeer. A historically significant maritime and trading nation, the Netherlands built a major colonial empire during its 17th-century Golden Age. Amsterdam is the constitutional capital while The Hague is the seat of government and home to the International Court of Justice.
subTitle: 12 Provinces

history.label: Red-white-blue tricolour
history.note: The modern red-white-blue continued through the Batavian Republic, Kingdom, and present day; its bright vermilion red was officially fixed by royal decree in 1937. Orange survives in the pennant flown above the flag on royal occasions.

history.label: Statenvlag (States Flag)
history.note: Orange was gradually swapped for red between roughly 1630 and 1660 — partly because red was more visible at sea, partly to signal the Republic's growing distance from the House of Orange. A 1664 resolution formally named the red-white-blue the "Statenvlag".

history.label: Prinsenvlag (Prince's Flag)
history.note: An orange-white-blue tricolour tied to William of Orange and the Dutch Revolt against Spain. It is the direct ancestor of today's flag and remains an emblem of the House of Orange.

predecessors.name: City of Gouda
predecessors.note: Red-and-white vertical stripes with three silver six-pointed stars — the civic banner of the Dutch cheese city of Gouda, of medieval origin.

predecessors.name: Batavian Republic
predecessors.note: The Dutch revolutionary republic's flag placed a white canton on the tricolor enclosing a seated liberty maiden with a liberty-cap spear and the Dutch lion.

predecessors.name: Dutch Republic
predecessors.note: The red-white-blue 'Statenvlag' of the Seven United Provinces, a merchant republic that became a 17th-century global trading and naval superpower in its Golden Age.

name: North Macedonia
overview: North Macedonia is a small landlocked country in the southern Balkans that was part of Yugoslavia until 1991. A prolonged dispute with Greece over the use of the name 'Macedonia' was resolved in 2019 when it officially became the Republic of North Macedonia. Skopje, the capital, was largely destroyed by a devastating earthquake in 1963 and was rebuilt over subsequent decades, and is home to one of the largest and oldest bazaars in the Balkans.
subTitle: 8 Regions

history.label: Flag of North Macedonia
history.note: Adopted in 1995 to end the dispute with Greece, it shows a stylized golden sun with eight broadening rays on red. The flag was unchanged by the 2019 renaming to "North Macedonia".

history.label: Star of Vergina flag
history.note: The first independence flag bore the golden 16-rayed Star of Vergina, an ancient Macedonian symbol. Greece considered it theft of Hellenic heritage and imposed a blockade until it was changed.

history.label: SR Macedonia
history.note: A plain red field with a gold-bordered red star in the canton — unusual among Yugoslav republics for being entirely red rather than a tricolor.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Yugoslavia
predecessors.note: The blue-white-red Pan-Slavic tricolor symbolized the union of Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes, flown by the Kingdom until the Axis invasion of 1941.

predecessors.name: Socialist Yugoslavia
predecessors.note: Tito's Partisans added a red, gold-bordered star to the tricolor; it marked the country's communist character until the federation's violent breakup in the 1990s.

predecessors.name: Ottoman Empire
predecessors.note: The red field with white crescent and star was standardized in 1844; older Ottoman banners varied widely and often carried multiple crescents.

name: Norway
overview: Norway is a Nordic country occupying the western part of the Scandinavian Peninsula, famous for its dramatic fjords, the aurora borealis, the midnight sun, and a remarkably high standard of living. The country manages one of the world's largest sovereign wealth funds, generated from its offshore oil and gas revenues. Norway has a strong tradition of international peacekeeping and humanitarian aid and consistently ranks among the best countries in the world for human development.
subTitle: 15 Counties

history.label: Norwegian Flag (Meltzer design)
history.note: Designed by merchant and politician Fredrik Meltzer, this flag cleverly incorporated the three colours of the French Tricolore (associated with liberty) into a Nordic cross — blue within white within red. The design was adopted by the Norwegian Storting in 1821, though Norway was in union with Sweden and the flag was slow to gain official recognition abroad.

history.label: First Norwegian Flag (Eidsvoll)
history.note: When Norway's constitution was signed at Eidsvoll in May 1814, the country briefly gained independence before entering a union with Sweden. This first flag — a red field with a simple white cross — was used during that brief window and inspired the later, more distinctive design.

history.label: Denmark–Norway
history.note: For nearly three centuries Norway was the junior partner in the kingdom of Denmark–Norway, ruled from Copenhagen and flying the Danish Dannebrog. When Denmark backed Napoleon and lost, Norway was handed to Sweden in 1814 — but not before Norwegians seized the moment to write their own constitution, the seed of the independence finally won in 1905.

predecessors.name: Kalmar Union
predecessors.note: The dynastic union that joined Denmark, Sweden, and Norway under a single monarch for over a century, until Gustav Vasa led Sweden to break away in 1523.

name: Poland
overview: Poland is the largest country in Central Europe by area and population, with a turbulent history shaped by centuries of partition and occupation by neighbouring empires. Warsaw, the capital, was almost entirely destroyed during World War II and was painstakingly rebuilt by its citizens, with the historic centre now a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Poland joined NATO in 1999 and the European Union in 2004 and has become one of Europe's fastest-growing economies.
subTitle: 16 Voivodeships

history.label: White-and-red bicolour
history.note: The Sejm officially adopted the horizontal white-over-red bicolour in 1919, and its basic design has been unchanged since — retained even throughout the communist People's Republic.

history.label: People's Republic state flag
history.note: The communist-era state ensign bore a white eagle stripped of its crown. The crown was restored to the eagle at the end of 1989 as communism fell.

history.label: Second Republic state flag
history.note: The interwar state ensign charged with the crowned white eagle of the reborn Polish Republic, before the coat of arms was redesigned in 1927–28.

history.label: Kingdom of Poland (royal banner)
history.note: Long before the modern white-and-red bicolor, the Kingdom of Poland — and the vast Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth it formed in 1569 — was represented by the royal banner: the crowned white eagle on a red field, in use since the late 13th century. The white-and-red of the national colours is drawn directly from this eagle. The Commonwealth, once one of Europe's largest states, was carved up and erased by Russia, Prussia and Austria in the partitions ending in 1795.

predecessors.name: Free City of Danzig
predecessors.note: Two white crosses and a gold crown on red, from the city's medieval arms. This League of Nations-administered free city was the flashpoint where World War II began.

predecessors.name: Lemko-Rusyn Republic
predecessors.note: A pro-Russian Rusyn republic centered on Florynka in the western Lemko region, flying a blue-yellow-green tricolor after the collapse of Austria-Hungary.

predecessors.name: Duchy of Warsaw
predecessors.note: Napoleon's Polish client state, a brief restoration of statehood after the Partitions; its white-and-crimson flag preceded the modern Polish flag by a century.

predecessors.name: Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth
predecessors.note: One of the largest states in Europe, an elective monarchy with an early constitution and a 'noble democracy', partitioned out of existence by Russia, Prussia, and Austria.

predecessors.name: Free City of Kraków
predecessors.note: A tiny independent city-republic created at the Congress of Vienna, the last fragment of sovereign Poland, until Austria annexed it after a failed 1846 uprising.

predecessors.name: Polish People's Republic
predecessors.note: The Soviet-aligned communist state flew the same white-and-red bicolor as the republic before and after it, the eagle on its arms shorn of its royal crown until 1989.

name: Portugal
overview: Portugal occupies the southwestern tip of continental Europe and was once the centre of one of history's most far-reaching maritime empires. Portuguese navigators in the 15th and 16th centuries opened sea routes to Africa, Asia, and the Americas, fundamentally reshaping global trade. Today Portugal is known for its fado music, azulejo tilework, pastel de nata custard tarts, port wine, and is one of the oldest nation-states in Europe.
subTitle: 20 Districts & Regions

history.label: Republican flag
history.note: Adopted in 1911 after the Republic was proclaimed, replacing royal blue-and-white with green and red. The arms sit on an armillary sphere recalling the Age of Discoveries, placed over the boundary of the two colors.

history.label: Constitutional Monarchy
history.note: A field split blue at the hoist and white at the fly, with the crowned royal arms over an armillary sphere on the dividing line. It flew until the revolution of 5 October 1910 overthrew the monarchy.

history.label: White flag with royal arms
history.note: A plain white field bearing the Portuguese royal coat of arms, used through the reigns of John V, Joseph I and Maria I. The white field carried no meaning of its own until colors were added in the 19th century.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Portugal
predecessors.note: The white-and-blue royal banner with the shield of five 'quinas' flew over the seafaring kingdom that launched the Age of Discovery, until the 1910 republican revolution.

predecessors.name: Iberian Union
predecessors.note: For sixty years the Spanish and Portuguese crowns were joined under one Habsburg king, briefly creating a single empire spanning the globe, until Portugal revolted in 1640.

name: Romania
overview: Romania is a country at the intersection of Central, Eastern, and Southeastern Europe, home to the legendary Transylvania region, the Carpathian Mountains, and the biodiverse Danube Delta. The country emerged dramatically from communist rule in December 1989, when Nicolae Ceaușescu's government was overthrown in the only violent revolution of the Eastern Bloc's 1989 wave. Romania joined the EU in 2007 and is known for its medieval citadels, painted monasteries of Bucovina, and unique folk traditions.
subTitle: 42 Counties

history.label: Current tricolor
history.note: Post-revolution Romania restored the plain blue-yellow-red tricolor without any emblem. The official cobalt blue, chrome yellow and vermilion red shades were later fixed by law.

history.label: 1989 Revolution 'flag with the hole'
history.note: During the December 1989 revolution, protesters cut the socialist emblem out of the flag's center, leaving a defiant hole. It became the iconic image of the overthrow of Ceaușescu.

history.label: Socialist Republic emblem
history.note: Under Ceaușescu the emblem was finalized with the inscription "Republica Socialistă România". This is the flag protesters would later mutilate in 1989.

history.label: People's Republic (with star)
history.note: A red five-pointed star was added atop the emblem during the high-Stalinist period, the arms bordered by a ribbon in the national colors.

history.label: People's Republic (first emblem)
history.note: The new communist republic added a coat of arms with a rising sun, oil derrick and wheat to the tricolor's center.

history.label: Kingdom of Romania
history.note: The plain blue-yellow-red vertical tricolor served the Romanian Old Kingdom and interwar monarchy. The colors trace back to the revolutions of 1848.

predecessors.name: Principality of Transylvania
predecessors.note: A largely autonomous Ottoman vassal principality and Protestant stronghold; its blue-and-gold flag joined the symbols of its Hungarian, Székely, and Saxon 'nations'.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Romania
predecessors.note: The blue-yellow-red tricolor of the Hohenzollern-Sigmaringen kings, who doubled the country's size after World War I before the communists forced King Michael to abdicate.

predecessors.name: Principality of Wallachia
predecessors.note: A Danubian principality long under Ottoman suzerainty, ruled by voivodes including Vlad the Impaler; it united with Moldavia in 1859 to form modern Romania.

predecessors.name: Principality of Moldavia
predecessors.note: The eastern Romanian principality of Stephen the Great, who held off the Ottomans for decades; its union with Wallachia created the United Principalities of Romania.

name: Russia
overview: Russia is the world's largest country by land area, spanning eleven time zones and stretching from Eastern Europe across northern Asia to the Pacific Ocean. Moscow, the capital, is one of Europe's largest cities and a major global political, economic, and cultural centre. Russia has an extraordinarily rich literary and artistic heritage, with writers like Tolstoy and Dostoevsky, composers like Tchaikovsky and Stravinsky, and a deep tradition of classical ballet.
subTitle: 85 Federal Subjects

history.label: Russian Federation
history.note: The white-blue-red tricolor was restored after the fall of the Soviet Union and fixed in its current 2:3 proportions by decree in 1993, then confirmed by federal law in 2000. The colors trace back to Peter the Great.

history.label: Russian Federation (1991 transitional)
history.note: When Russia re-adopted the historic tricolor in 1991 the first version used a slightly lighter shade and longer 1:2 proportions, before the design was standardized two years later.

history.label: Soviet Union
history.note: The red banner with a gold hammer, sickle, and star symbolized the union of workers and peasants. It flew over the USSR for nearly seven decades until the country dissolved in December 1991.

history.label: Far Eastern Republic
history.note: A nominally independent buffer state created by Soviet Russia in 1920 to avoid direct war with Japan, which occupied parts of the Russian Far East during the Civil War. Its red flag bore a blue canton lettered "DVR." Once Japanese forces withdrew in 1922, the republic dissolved itself into Soviet Russia.

history.label: Russian Empire (imperial colors)
history.note: Decreed by Alexander II in 1858, the black-yellow-white flag drew on the heraldic colors of the imperial coat of arms. It served as the official state flag for nearly four decades before the tricolor was restored.

history.label: Tsardom of Russia (Petrine tricolor)
history.note: Peter the Great introduced the white-blue-red tricolor in the 1690s for the merchant fleet, modeled on the Dutch flag. Restored again in 1896, it is the direct ancestor of the modern Russian flag.

history.label: Tuvan People's Republic
history.note: A nominally independent Soviet-aligned state in southern Siberia that was quietly annexed into the USSR in 1944.

history.label: Confederated Republic of Altai
history.note: A short-lived attempt at an autonomous Altai state in the Russian Civil War's Siberian chaos.

history.label: Uryankhay Krai
history.note: The Russian protectorate over Tuva, between Mongolia and Siberia, before the Tuvan People's Republic.

predecessors.name: Kuban People's Republic
predecessors.note: An anti-Bolshevik Cossack state on the Kuban river; its blue-raspberry-green bands stood for Cossacks, non-Cossack residents, and highlanders.

predecessors.name: Don Republic
predecessors.note: The Don Cossack state's blue-yellow-red tricolor stood for the Don Cossacks, Kalmyks, and Russian non-Cossack residents during the Russian Civil War.

predecessors.name: Mountainous Republic of the Northern Caucasus
predecessors.note: Green and white stripes with a blue canton bearing seven stars for the seven main highland peoples of the North Caucasus.

predecessors.name: North Caucasian Emirate
predecessors.note: A short-lived theocratic emirate under Uzun Hajji spanning Chechnya and Dagestan during the Russian Civil War; its green flag bears three white stars above a crescent.

predecessors.name: Far Eastern Republic
predecessors.note: A nominally independent buffer state the Bolsheviks set up across Siberia to avoid war with Japan; once the Japanese withdrew, it was absorbed into Soviet Russia.

predecessors.name: Tuvan People's Republic
predecessors.note: A tiny Soviet-aligned republic in southern Siberia, recognized only by the USSR and Mongolia; its famous triangular stamps were prized by collectors before the USSR annexed it in 1944.

predecessors.name: Russian SFSR
predecessors.note: The largest Soviet republic flew a red flag with a thin blue stripe down the hoist; the RSFSR was the core of the USSR and became the Russian Federation in 1991.

predecessors.name: Kazan Khanate
predecessors.note: A Tatar successor state of the Golden Horde on the Volga, conquered by Ivan the Terrible in 1552 — a turning point in Russia's eastward expansion.

predecessors.name: Khanate of Sibir
predecessors.note: The Turco-Mongol khanate beyond the Urals that gave Siberia its name; its conquest by the Cossack Yermak opened Russia's vast eastward expansion across Asia.

predecessors.name: Khanate of Kazan
predecessors.note: A Tatar successor state of the Golden Horde on the Volga; Ivan the Terrible's bloody siege and capture of Kazan in 1552 is commemorated by Moscow's St Basil's Cathedral.

predecessors.name: Khanate of Astrakhan
predecessors.note: A small Tatar khanate guarding the Volga delta and the Caspian trade, the last of the Golden Horde's successors, annexed by Ivan the Terrible four years after Kazan.

predecessors.name: Golden Horde
predecessors.note: The Mongol khanate that held the Russian principalities under the 'Tatar Yoke' for over two centuries; this white banner with a crescent appears on a 1339 Genoese map.

predecessors.name: Idel-Ural State
predecessors.note: A short-lived Tatar-Bashkir republic on the Volga proclaimed amid the Russian Revolution, crushed by the Bolsheviks within weeks of its declaration.

predecessors.name: Green Ukraine
predecessors.note: An attempt by Ukrainian settlers in the Russian Far East to form an autonomous state around the Amur and Pacific coast, suppressed by the Bolsheviks.

name: San Marino
overview: San Marino is a landlocked microstate entirely surrounded by Italy, and according to tradition was founded in 301 AD, making it the world's oldest republic still in existence. Its territory consists of the slopes of Mount Titano, topped by three medieval fortified towers that have overlooked the surrounding Italian plain for centuries. Despite being completely surrounded by Italian territory, San Marino maintains its own government, passport, and distinct national identity.
subTitle: 9 Municipalities

history.label: White-blue with arms
history.note: A Constitutional Law of 2011 definitively fixed the flag and slightly refined the arms. White stands for peace and blue for liberty; the arms show three towers on Monte Titano beneath the motto LIBERTAS.

history.label: White-blue with arms (1862)
history.note: The white-over-light-blue flag with the central coat of arms was standardized by decree in 1862 and flew almost unchanged for nearly 150 years.

name: Serbia
overview: Serbia is a landlocked Balkan country that was historically the dominant republic within the former Yugoslavia. Belgrade, the capital, sits at the confluence of the Sava and Danube rivers and has been an important regional hub for thousands of years — it has been destroyed and rebuilt more than 40 times in its history. Serbia is known for its vibrant music scene, including the Exit Festival, and a rich Eastern Orthodox Christian heritage with important medieval monasteries.
subTitle: Province & Districts

history.label: Flag of Serbia
history.note: A 2010 standardization set the coat of arms in its present form — the double-headed white eagle and the cross with four firesteels (the "Serbian cross").

history.label: Tricolor with arms (2004)
history.note: In 2004 Serbia restored the double-headed eagle and shield, reviving royal-era symbolism abandoned under socialism.

history.label: Plain red-blue-white
history.note: After the communist era Serbia simply dropped the star, leaving a bare tricolor that carried no coat of arms throughout the Milošević years.

history.label: SR Serbia
history.note: The Socialist Republic flew a red-blue-white tricolor with a centered gold-bordered red star — visually identical to the flag of SR Montenegro.

history.label: Kingdom of Serbia
history.note: When Serbia became a kingdom in 1882, its state flag was the red-blue-white tricolor charged with the royal coat of arms — the double-headed eagle beneath a crown. This is the direct ancestor of the modern flag; the kingdom dissolved into Yugoslavia in 1918.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Yugoslavia
predecessors.note: The blue-white-red Pan-Slavic tricolor symbolized the union of Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes, flown by the Kingdom until the Axis invasion of 1941.

predecessors.name: Socialist Yugoslavia
predecessors.note: Tito's Partisans added a red, gold-bordered star to the tricolor; it marked the country's communist character until the federation's violent breakup in the 1990s.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Serbia
predecessors.note: The red-blue-white tricolor with the royal arms flew over the independent Serbian kingdom whose alliances and crisis with Austria-Hungary helped ignite World War I.

predecessors.name: Principality of Serbia
predecessors.note: An autonomous principality that won self-rule from the Ottomans under the rival Obrenović and Karađorđević dynasties before being raised to a kingdom in 1882.

predecessors.name: Serbian Despotate
predecessors.note: The last independent medieval Serbian state, a despotate that briefly flourished at Smederevo before the Ottomans conquered it, ending Serbian statehood for centuries.

name: Slovakia
overview: Slovakia is a landlocked Central European country sharing borders with Austria, Czech Republic, Hungary, Poland, and Ukraine. It was joined with the Czech Republic in Czechoslovakia until the two nations amicably separated in 1993 in the Velvet Divorce. Bratislava, the capital, is uniquely positioned so that residents can reach three different countries — Slovakia, Austria, and Hungary — within an hour's drive, making it one of Europe's most internationally connected capitals.
subTitle: 8 Regions

history.label: Slovak flag (with arms)
history.note: Adopted in September 1992 before the split from Czechoslovakia, it shifts the national arms (a double cross on three hills) toward the hoist so the flag is not confused with other Slavic tricolors.

history.label: Plain Pan-Slavic tricolor
history.note: The WWII First Slovak Republic flew a plain white-blue-red tricolor identical to several other Slavic flags. The same plain design was briefly readopted in 1990–1992 before arms were added.

history.label: Part of Czechoslovakia
history.note: Apart from the WWII puppet state, Slovakia spent most of the 20th century as the eastern half of Czechoslovakia (1918–1939 and 1945–1992), flying the Czechoslovak flag with its blue hoist triangle. Slovak frustration at being the junior partner drove the "Velvet Divorce" of 1993 — the peaceful split that gave Slovakia its own arms-bearing flag.

predecessors.name: Austria-Hungary
predecessors.note: The civil ensign split 50/50, joining the Austrian red-white-red with Hungary's colors and both shields — the Dual Monarchy never agreed on a single flag for the whole empire.

predecessors.name: Czechoslovakia
predecessors.note: Adopted in 1920 with a blue hoist wedge to tell it apart from Poland's flag. Czechia kept it after the 1993 split, despite a clause meant to bar successor states from reusing it.

predecessors.name: First Slovak Republic
predecessors.note: A clerical-nationalist client state of Nazi Germany under Jozef Tiso, the first time Slovakia existed as a separate state; it flew a plain white-blue-red tricolor.

name: Slovenia
overview: Slovenia is one of the smallest and most prosperous countries in Central Europe, known for its alpine terrain, pristine lakes, medieval old towns, and remarkable biodiversity packed into a small area. It was the first former Yugoslav republic to join the European Union in 2004. Lake Bled, with its island church and clifftop castle reflected in turquoise glacial waters, is one of Europe's most photographed and widely recognised landscapes.
subTitle: 12 Regions

history.label: Flag of Slovenia
history.note: Adopted at independence in June 1991, it replaced the red star with the national arms in the upper hoist: Mount Triglav, wavy lines for sea and rivers, and three gold stars of the Counts of Celje.

history.label: SR Slovenia
history.note: The Socialist Republic used a white-blue-red tricolor with a gold-bordered red star centered on it.

history.label: Carniola (Slovene lands)
history.note: The Slovene white-blue-red tricolor was born in 1848, when Slovene nationalists adopted the colours of the Duchy of Carniola (Krain) — the historic Slovene heartland within the Habsburg Empire. It was the first distinctly Slovene flag, raised in defiance of Austrian-German rule, and is the direct ancestor of every Slovenian flag since.

predecessors.name: Austria-Hungary
predecessors.note: The civil ensign split 50/50, joining the Austrian red-white-red with Hungary's colors and both shields — the Dual Monarchy never agreed on a single flag for the whole empire.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Yugoslavia
predecessors.note: The blue-white-red Pan-Slavic tricolor symbolized the union of Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes, flown by the Kingdom until the Axis invasion of 1941.

predecessors.name: Socialist Yugoslavia
predecessors.note: Tito's Partisans added a red, gold-bordered star to the tricolor; it marked the country's communist character until the federation's violent breakup in the 1990s.

predecessors.name: County of Gorizia (Görz)
predecessors.note: A white-and-red bicolour for the Habsburg princely county on the Adriatic frontier, whose arms feature a gold lion. Its land is now split between Italy and Slovenia.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Illyria
predecessors.note: An Austrian crown land carved from former Napoleonic territory along the Adriatic, covering parts of modern Slovenia, Croatia, and Austria.

name: Spain
overview: Spain is the second-largest country in the European Union by area, situated on the Iberian Peninsula with additional territory in the Canary Islands, Balearic Islands, and two cities on the North African coast. It has a rich cultural heritage blending Roman, Moorish, and Christian traditions, visible in its architecture, art, cuisine, and festivals. Spain has more UNESCO World Heritage Sites than almost any other country and is one of the world's most visited nations.
subTitle: 19 Regions

history.label: Kingdom of Spain
history.note: The current flag, fixed in 1981, bears the constitutional coat of arms with the Pillars of Hercules and the quartered arms of Castile, León, Aragón, and Navarre. The red-yellow-red scheme dates to 1785.

history.label: Spain (Transition)
history.note: After Franco's death the eagle of the dictatorship's flag was widened and the design softened, a transitional step while the new democratic constitution of 1978 took shape.

history.label: Francoist Spain
history.note: Franco's flag restored the red-yellow-red bands and added the Eagle of Saint John bearing the medieval royal arms with the yoke-and-arrows of the Catholic Monarchs. It flew for most of his dictatorship.

history.label: Second Spanish Republic
history.note: The Republic replaced the red-yellow-red with a tricolour of red, yellow, and murrey (purple), the purple honouring the Castilian Comuneros. It was abolished by Franco's victory in the Civil War.

history.label: Kingdom of Spain (Bourbon)
history.note: In 1785 Charles III chose the now-iconic red-yellow-red design as a high-visibility naval ensign, later extended to land. This basic scheme persisted through the monarchy and Bourbon Restoration.

history.label: Cross of Burgundy
history.note: A red knotted saltire on a white field served as the de facto flag of the Spanish Empire under the Habsburgs and early Bourbons, flying over its global possessions and armies for nearly three centuries.

predecessors.name: Cross of Burgundy
predecessors.note: The red saw-tooth saltire represents two crossed, roughly-pruned branches. It flew over Spanish armies and colonies across the Americas for nearly three centuries.

predecessors.name: Second Spanish Republic
predecessors.note: The republican tricolor added a purple lower band popularly linked to Castile, replacing the red-yellow monarchist flag until Franco's victory in 1939.

predecessors.name: Couto Misto
predecessors.note: A self-governing 'mixed county' of three villages on the Galicia–Portugal border with its own asylum rights, partitioned between Spain and Portugal in 1864.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Galicia
predecessors.note: One of medieval Europe's oldest kingdoms, in Spain's far northwest; its modern white flag with a blue diagonal recalls the realm home to the pilgrim shrine of Santiago de Compostela.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Navarre
predecessors.note: The Basque Pyrenean kingdom whose red flag bears golden chains, said to commemorate Navarrese knights breaking the chains around the Almohad caliph's tent in 1212.

predecessors.name: Crown of Aragon
predecessors.note: The four red bars on gold — the Senyera — flew over a Mediterranean empire that ruled Catalonia, Valencia, the Balearics, Sardinia, Sicily, and at times Naples and Athens.

predecessors.name: Principality of Catalonia
predecessors.note: The Senyera of four red bars on gold flew over the Catalan lands within the Crown of Aragon; Barcelona's fall in 1714 ended its self-government, an event still marked each September 11.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of León
predecessors.note: The purple lion on white of the early medieval Christian kingdom that drove the Reconquista; its parliament of 1188 is often called the oldest in Europe.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Asturias
predecessors.note: The blue flag with the gold Victory Cross marks the first Christian kingdom of the Reconquista, founded by Pelagius after his victory at Covadonga against the Moors.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Majorca
predecessors.note: A short-lived independent Balearic kingdom carved out by James I of Aragon for a younger son, ruling the islands plus Roussillon and Montpellier on the mainland.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Valencia
predecessors.note: A realm of the Crown of Aragon whose Senyera added a crowned blue stripe at the hoist; it was abolished by the Bourbons after backing the losing side in the War of Spanish Succession.

predecessors.name: Iberian Union
predecessors.note: For sixty years the Spanish and Portuguese crowns were joined under one Habsburg king, briefly creating a single empire spanning the globe, until Portugal revolted in 1640.

name: Sweden
overview: Sweden is the largest country in Northern Europe and is known for its progressive social model, high standard of living, and a long tradition of neutrality in international conflicts. It is home to the Nobel Prize, awarded annually in Stockholm, and has produced globally influential exports including IKEA, Spotify, Volvo, ABBA, and a remarkable tradition of children's literature. Sweden joined the European Union in 1995 and has one of the world's highest rates of gender equality.
subTitle: 21 Counties

history.label: Swedish Flag (colours standardised)
history.note: In 1906 a royal decree fixed the exact shades of blue and yellow, ending a century of variation. The Nordic cross design itself stretches back to at least the early 16th century — one of the oldest unchanged national symbols in Europe.

history.label: Swedish Flag with Union Mark
history.note: During the personal union with Norway (1814–1905), a combined canton showing both national crosses appeared in the upper hoist corner. Sweden and Norway shared a king but remained separate kingdoms; the union mark was a constant reminder of that political arrangement.

history.label: Classic Swedish Flag
history.note: The golden Nordic cross on blue is virtually unchanged from the earliest recorded use under Gustav Vasa in the 1520s. Sweden is one of the very few countries whose flag design has remained stable for five centuries.

predecessors.name: Kalmar Union
predecessors.note: The dynastic union that joined Denmark, Sweden, and Norway under a single monarch for over a century, until Gustav Vasa led Sweden to break away in 1523.

predecessors.name: Scanian Lands
predecessors.note: A red-and-yellow Nordic-cross flag for the southern provinces that were Danish for centuries before Sweden conquered them in 1658 — a frontier fought over for generations.

name: Switzerland
overview: Switzerland is a landlocked country in Central Europe famous for its stunning alpine scenery, precision watchmaking, chocolate, cheese, and centuries of political neutrality. The Swiss Confederation, founded in 1291, is one of the world's oldest federations and has maintained armed neutrality in European conflicts since 1815. Switzerland is home to the International Red Cross (founded there in 1863), numerous United Nations agencies, and has four official national languages: German, French, Italian, and Romansh.
subTitle: 26 Cantons

history.label: Swiss Federal Flag (standardised)
history.note: Switzerland officially standardised its federal flag in 1889 — a bold white cross on red, in a square format unique among sovereign nations (only Vatican City also uses a square flag). The equal-armed cross has represented the Swiss Confederation since at least the Battle of Laupen in 1339, when Swiss troops wore white crosses as field insignia. The Red Cross flag is its inverse — white field, red cross — created in 1863 by Swiss founder Henry Dunant as a deliberate tribute.

history.label: Restored Confederation
history.note: After the fall of Napoleon, the 1815 Federal Treaty restored the Swiss Confederation and revived the white cross on red as its emblem. The proportions and exact shade of red still varied between cantons for decades before the square national flag was formally standardised in 1889.

history.label: Helvetic Republic
history.note: When France invaded in 1798 and swept away the old confederation, the short-lived Helvetic Republic adopted a completely different flag — a horizontal green-red-yellow tricolor, the first ever single national flag for all of Switzerland. It abandoned the centuries-old cross entirely. The republic collapsed by 1803, and the cross returned with the restored confederation.

history.label: Medieval Origins
history.note: Swiss soldiers wore white crosses on red surcoats at the Battle of Laupen (1339) to identify themselves as a confederation. The cross appeared on cantonal battle flags long before Switzerland had a formal national banner, flying over the loose alliance of cantons until the French invasion of 1798.

predecessors.name: Rhodanic Republic
predecessors.note: The Napoleonic-era republic of Valais flew a vertical red-and-white field with stars for its districts, before being absorbed directly into France.

predecessors.name: Republic of Geneva
predecessors.note: An independent Calvinist city-republic — the 'Protestant Rome' — until France annexed it; it later joined the Swiss Confederation as a canton in 1815.

predecessors.name: Old Swiss Confederacy
predecessors.note: The loose alliance of cantons that grew from the legendary 1291 oath; the white cross on red it fought under at Morgarten and Sempach became the modern Swiss flag.

name: Ukraine
overview: Ukraine is the largest country situated entirely within Europe, a vast nation with a rich agricultural tradition — it was once called the 'breadbasket of Europe' — and significant mineral and industrial resources. Kyiv, one of Eastern Europe's oldest and most historic cities, was the capital of the medieval Kyivan Rus state, a major cultural and political centre of early medieval Europe. Ukraine declared independence from the Soviet Union in 1991 and has a population of around 40 million people.
subTitle: 27 Oblasts

history.label: Flag of Ukraine
history.note: The blue-and-yellow bicolour was restored at independence in 1991, reviving the 1917 design. It is widely read as blue sky above golden wheat fields.

history.label: Ukrainian SSR
history.note: A red flag with a light-blue band along the bottom third and a gold hammer, sickle, and star. The blue stripe set it apart from other Soviet republic flags.

history.label: Ukrainian People's Republic
history.note: The blue-over-yellow bicolour served the short-lived Ukrainian People's Republic during the revolutionary period, drawing on Ukrainian heraldic tradition.

history.label: Cossack Hetmanate
history.note: The Cossack Hetmanate, founded by Bohdan Khmelnytsky in 1649, was a self-governing Cossack state that is widely regarded as a forerunner of Ukrainian statehood. Its banners were typically crimson with a cross and Cossack devices. Squeezed between Poland, the Ottomans and Muscovy, it was gradually absorbed by the Russian Empire, which abolished it in 1764.

predecessors.name: Russian Empire
predecessors.note: The black-yellow-white 'imperial colors' were drawn from the state coat of arms by Alexander II, before Nicholas II demoted them for the white-blue-red tricolor.

predecessors.name: Soviet Union
predecessors.note: The gold hammer and sickle beneath a red star stood for the worker-peasant alliance. The exact shade and proportions were fixed by Soviet law in 1955.

predecessors.name: Free Territory (Makhnovshchina)
predecessors.note: The Ukrainian anarchist movement under Nestor Makhno flew black banners reading 'Liberty or Death'. The popular skull-and-crossbones version is a debunked Soviet-propaganda myth.

predecessors.name: Cossack Hetmanate
predecessors.note: The Cossack state of central Ukraine, founded by Bohdan Khmelnytsky's uprising, flew a crimson banner with a Cossack bearing a musket.

predecessors.name: Donets–Krivoy Rog Soviet Republic
predecessors.note: A short-lived Bolshevik republic in the Donbas industrial basin that tried to stay part of Soviet Russia rather than join Ukraine — dissolved within months.

predecessors.name: Crimean People's Republic
predecessors.note: One of the first secular Muslim-led republics in the world, flying the sky-blue Kök Bayraq with a golden Tatar tamga, before the Bolsheviks crushed it within months.

predecessors.name: Ukrainian SSR
predecessors.note: A red flag with a lower azure band and a gold hammer, sickle and star, flown by Soviet Ukraine — which, unusually, held its own seat at the United Nations.

predecessors.name: Crimean Khanate
predecessors.note: A blue flag with the golden Giray-dynasty tamga; the last remnant of the Golden Horde, it dominated the northern Black Sea steppe until Catherine the Great annexed it.

name: United Kingdom
overview: The United Kingdom consists of four constituent nations — England, Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland — and was historically the centre of the world's largest empire, covering approximately a quarter of Earth's land surface at its peak. London, the capital, is one of the world's leading financial, cultural, and political cities, home to institutions from the British Museum to the Houses of Parliament. The UK is a founding member of NATO and a permanent member of the UN Security Council.
subTitle: 74 Regions & Districts

history.label: Union Jack (Current)
history.note: Created in 1801 when Ireland joined Great Britain. Combines St George's Cross (England), St Andrew's Cross (Scotland), and St Patrick's Cross (Ireland). The red diagonal is deliberately offset so the Scottish white saltire is not obscured.

history.label: Flag of Great Britain (1707–1800)
history.note: Created by the Acts of Union 1707 joining England and Scotland. Combined only St George's Cross and St Andrew's Cross. Ireland had not yet joined, so there is no red diagonal of St Patrick.

history.label: King's Colours (Union of the Crowns)
history.note: When James VI of Scotland also became James I of England in 1603, the two crowns were united under one king though the kingdoms stayed separate. In 1606 he ordered this first Union Flag — St George over St Andrew — flown at sea. It served as a royal and maritime banner for a century before the 1707 union made it the flag of the new Kingdom of Great Britain.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Mann
predecessors.note: The gold three-legged triskelion on red descends from the Norse-Gaelic Kingdom of Mann and the Isles, which once ruled the Irish Sea from the Isle of Man.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of England
predecessors.note: The red St George's Cross on white became England's flag in the late Middle Ages; the kingdom merged with Scotland in 1707 to form Great Britain.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Scotland
predecessors.note: The white Saltire of St Andrew on blue, one of the oldest national flags in the world, flew over independent Scotland until the union with England in 1707.

predecessors.name: Kingdom of Great Britain
predecessors.note: The first Union Flag, combining the crosses of St George and St Andrew, flew over the merged kingdom until Ireland's 1801 union added St Patrick's saltire to make the modern flag.

predecessors.name: Commonwealth of England
predecessors.note: England's brief republic after the execution of Charles I, ruled by Oliver Cromwell as Lord Protector; its flag combined the crosses of England, Scotland, and Ireland with his arms.

name: Vatican City
overview: Vatican City is the world's smallest internationally recognised country, an independent city-state entirely within Rome covering just 44 hectares. It serves as the headquarters of the Roman Catholic Church and the residence of the Pope, giving this tiny territory immense religious significance for over 1.3 billion Catholics worldwide. Vatican City is home to St Peter's Basilica, the Sistine Chapel with Michelangelo's famous ceiling fresco, and some of the world's most important art and historical collections.
subTitle: 4 Areas

history.label: Yellow-white with keys and tiara
history.note: The 2023 Fundamental Law accompanied a minor revision of the emblem. The flag remains one of only two square national flags in the world, alongside Switzerland.

history.label: Original Vatican flag (1929)
history.note: Adopted with the 1929 Lateran Treaty that created Vatican City, the square yellow-and-white bicolor bears the crossed keys of St. Peter and the papal tiara, derived from the flag of the Papal States.

history.label: Papal States (yellow-white)
history.note: For over a thousand years the popes ruled a sovereign state across central Italy. In 1808 its flag changed from yellow-and-red to the gold-and-white that still flies over the Vatican — the gold and silver of the keys of Saint Peter. The Papal States were swallowed by a unifying Italy in 1870, leaving the popes stateless until the 1929 Lateran Treaty created tiny Vatican City.

history.label: Papal States (yellow-red)
history.note: Before 1808 the Papal States flew yellow-and-red — the traditional colours of the senate and people of Rome, inherited from antiquity. Napoleon's occupation prompted the switch to the gold-and-white that became the modern papal colours.

predecessors.name: Papal States
predecessors.note: The yellow-and-white vertical bicolor with the crossed keys of St Peter beneath the papal tiara is the direct ancestor of today's Vatican City flag.
